_meta.description: Single source of truth for the Prosper Innovation Growth study system. Contains fund-level narratives, district metadata + storytelling intros, and per-ticker data. Edit this file to update the guide; re-run scripts/build_guide.py to regenerate the HTML.

story: The companies in this portfolio are building the things underneath the AI and innovation narrative — the chips and cables and cooling systems, the power plants and transmission lines, the specialty metals and circuit boards, the drones and satellites, the diagnostic tests and rare-disease therapies, the trade schools training the welders, the contractors wiring the buildings. Most people have never heard of them. That is part of the point: in the gap between what the market has already priced and what these companies will become lies the opportunity we are looking for.

Taken together, these are not isolated bets. They are a connected vision of where innovation is taking us — a city we are building piece by piece. It starts with Components: The Chips, The Wires, and The Machine Shop, where raw materials and parts become the building blocks of everything else. Then we Build & Power it: The Construction Yards turn empty land into real facilities, and The Power Plant generates the electricity (and owns the sites) that bring it all to life. Money & Software run the city: The Financial District moves the capital that funds everything, and The Software Layer runs the code beneath it all. Then come The Services — what the city actually does for people. The Arsenal defends it and extends it into space. The Clinic catches disease early and The Research Labs treats what once had no treatment. And Main Street is where people live their lives — shopping, eating, traveling, training, and being entertained.
Every company in this fund is playing a role. This guide is how to remember all of them.

Focused Large Cap is the highest-conviction expression of the City of the Future. Around 34 names — every one of them earning its place. The portfolio leans into the megaliths that anchor the AI buildout (NVIDIA, Broadcom, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, Apple, Amazon), the dominant pharmaceutical innovators (Eli Lilly, Gilead), and a tight collection of mid-cap names where conviction is unusually high (Bloom Energy, GE Vernova, Howmet, argenx).

Every district is represented but the weight is intentional: The Chips and The Software Layer dominate, because that is where the AI revolution is being built and monetized. The Research Labs holds a select few biotech leaders rather than a broad biotech bet. The Construction Yards, The Power Plant, and The Machine Shop carry the picks-and-shovels exposure to the data center buildout. Every name here is meant to be recognizable — to a client, to a teammate, to a board.
If the broader funds in the complex are about discovering tomorrow's leaders, Focused Large Cap is about owning today's. This is the guide for the highest-conviction core.

Large Cap is the broadest expression of the City of the Future at scale. Around 60 names that span every major district, with a deliberate tilt toward proven business models, durable franchises, and the megaliths that have already won their categories. Where Focused Large Cap is concentrated and high-conviction, Large Cap takes a more diversified view of the same thesis.

The Chips and The Software Layer carry the AI buildout — NVIDIA, Broadcom, AMD, Micron, Lam Research, KLA, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, Cloudflare, CrowdStrike, Palantir. The Research Labs holds the major pharmaceutical innovators (Eli Lilly, Vertex, Gilead, argenx) plus a few rare-disease specialists. The Power Plant and Construction Yards capture the physical infrastructure required to make AI real (GE Vernova, Cheniere, Vertiv, EMCOR). The Arsenal owns the defense and aerospace primes (L3Harris, GE Aerospace, Howmet) and Main Street carries the consumer giants (Apple, Amazon, Costco, Tesla, Netflix, Hilton).
This is the workhorse fund — the one a typical investor would own as a single core large-cap allocation in growth-oriented innovation.

Mid Cap is where some of the most interesting stories in the City of the Future actually live. Around 80 names that have grown beyond small cap but have not yet been crowned by the index funds. This is where the next decade's large caps come from — and where the alpha tends to compound when you get the picks right.

The fund is heavy on companies that own a specific niche and are scaling fast: CAVA in Mediterranean fast casual, AppLovin in mobile ad targeting, DoorDash in food delivery, Snowflake in cloud data, Datadog in observability, Live Nation in live entertainment, MercadoLibre in Latin American commerce. The Research Labs is loaded with mid-cap biotechs whose drugs are either approved or in late-stage trials (Madrigal, BridgeBio, Insmed, argenx). The Construction Yards and Power Plant capture the AI buildout names that are larger than small-cap pure plays but not yet mega-caps (EMCOR, Quanta, Vertiv, Cheniere). The Arsenal owns the next wave of defense modernization (Axon, Kratos, Karman).
Many of these are names you can show a client and say 'we owned this when nobody had heard of it.' The mid-cap guide is the one where conviction over time pays the most.

Micro Cap is where the venture-style bets live. Around 80 names that are still small enough to be unknown — many under $2 billion in market cap — and many of them in the early innings of their stories. The risk is real and the volatility is real. The upside, when a thesis works, is also real.

Several themes run through the portfolio. The Clinic is unusually deep here, with companies redefining how disease is detected: GeneDx in pediatric whole-genome sequencing, BillionToOne in molecular prenatal and oncology testing, CeriBell in point-of-care EEG, HeartFlow in non-invasive cardiac imaging, Twist Bioscience in synthetic DNA, 10x Genomics in single-cell sequencing. The Research Labs holds early-stage biotechs that could be the next Madrigal or Revolution Medicines if the science works out — Mineralys, NewAmsterdam, Praxis, Forte. The Arsenal owns the small defense and space names with outsized growth (Ondas, BlackSky, AEVEX, York Space Systems) and the Power Plant has the hardest-to-replicate clean energy and nuclear plays (Cipher, TeraWulf).

Many micro caps in this fund will not become mid caps. That is the nature of the asset class. The ones that do tend to compound at rates you cannot find anywhere else, and the guide exists to help remember why each one is in the portfolio in the first place.

chip_works.title: The Chips
chip_works.subtitle: Where silicon begins — and where rare earths come from
intro: Every city starts with a foundry. In this one, the foundry makes silicon. Modern chips have features measured in billionths of a meter — structures so small that a single virus would tower over them like a skyscraper. Building something at that scale requires extraordinary precision at every step, and the Chips is the district where it happens.

It begins in the ground. MP Materials (MP) mines and processes rare-earth elements — the minerals that create the powerful permanent magnets inside electric car motors, wind turbines, fighter jets, and chip-making equipment itself. MP is the only large-scale rare-earth producer in the Western Hemisphere, which makes it a strategic asset as well as a commercial one.

Inside the fab, Nova (NVMI) builds the inspection tools that photograph microscopic chip layers with beams of light and electrons, catching defects invisible to any human eye. MKS Instruments (MKSI) controls the gases, temperatures, and energy inside the fabrication chambers — imagine maintaining perfect weather inside a sealed room where a single stray particle could ruin everything. Amkor (AMKR) handles the final step, taking a finished chip thinner than a fingernail and encasing it in a protective housing with hundreds of tiny electrical connections so it can survive the heat and vibration of a working computer. Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) makes programmable chips that can be reconfigured after installation — a toolbox that transforms into whatever tool you need at the moment. And Rambus (RMBS) designs the rules that govern how processors and memory chips talk to each other, which is another way of saying Rambus decides how fast the entire computer can think. Without these six companies, the AI revolution would be a slideshow.

wires.title: The Wires
wires.subtitle: The interconnect that lets the city talk to itself
intro: A chip by itself is useless. Chips only matter when they can talk to each other — and in the era of AI, talking means moving vast amounts of data at extraordinary speeds between racks, between buildings, between cities, and across oceans. The Wires is the district where signals travel.

Credo Technology (CRDO) builds the high-speed cables and connection chips that let thousands of AI processors pass notes at billions of cycles per second. Picture a room full of brilliant people trying to solve a problem together, but they can only communicate by passing notes — the speed of those notes determines how fast the group can think. Credo makes the notes move at light speed. Astera Labs (ALAB) controls the data flows inside a single rack the way traffic cops manage a highway interchange, preventing collisions and making sure nothing gets stuck. MACOM (MTSI) turns electrical signals into laser light and back again so data can ride glass fibers across continents, billions of times per second.

SiTime (SITM) makes the precision silicon clocks that keep every signal perfectly synchronized — the conductor's baton inside every electronic orchestra. Semtech (SMTC) cleans up degraded signals so data arrives crisp instead of fuzzy, like preventing a photocopy-of-a-photocopy from losing quality with every pass. And Allegro MicroSystems (ALGM) builds the sensors that measure exactly how fast things are moving inside EVs, factory robots, and data-center cooling fans so control systems can respond in milliseconds. None of these companies make headlines. All of them are essential.

machine_shop.title: The Machine Shop
machine_shop.subtitle: Precision parts, specialty metals, and the hidden hands that assemble the city
intro: Chips and wires are useful only if someone can turn them into physical things — airplanes, satellites, medical devices, data-center cooling loops, circuit boards, power supplies. The Machine Shop is the district where raw materials and components become real machines. These are the hidden hands that turn brilliant designs into actual products.

Carpenter Technology (CRS) forges specialty metals and advanced alloys engineered at the atomic level to withstand 2,000-degree temperatures, corrosive chemicals, and thousands of pounds of pressure. These go into jet-engine turbine blades, surgical implants, and nuclear reactor components — applications where a material failure isn't an inconvenience but a catastrophe. RBC Bearings (RBC) makes the precision bearings that enable smooth rotation inside helicopter rotors, submarine propulsion systems, and industrial machinery — a deceptively simple concept, engineered to survive where nothing else can.

Sanmina (SANM) handles complex electronic contract manufacturing: the densely packed circuit boards inside medical imaging machines, the communication systems inside military vehicles, the server boards that run cloud computing. Fabrinet (FN) specializes in optical components aligned to tolerances thinner than a wavelength of light. TTM Technologies (TTMI) is one of the top five PCB manufacturers in the world and the largest supplier of printed circuit boards to the U.S. military, and the fastest-growing segment of its business is now data center computing and AI accelerators. Modine Manufacturing (MOD) designs the liquid cooling systems that absorb heat from AI racks the way a car radiator cools an engine — transforming a legacy auto-parts company into an AI infrastructure play. And Bel Fuse (BELFB) makes the power supplies and connectivity components that quietly sit inside almost every piece of essential equipment.

The last group is about control. Woodward (WWD) designs the fuel-metering control systems inside aircraft engines and industrial turbines — what happens when a pilot pushes the throttle forward is Woodward's responsibility. Crane Company (CR) supplies the engineered valves, sensors, and fluid-handling systems used in aircraft, naval vessels, and factories. And Littelfuse (LFUS) makes circuit-protection components that sacrifice themselves during power surges, absorbing excess energy before expensive equipment is damaged. Every physical machine in the city passes through a shop like one of these.

power_plant.title: The Power Plant
power_plant.subtitle: Where the electrons come from — and where they get used
intro: Intelligence without electricity is just an expensive pile of metal. And the amount of electricity that AI requires is almost hard to believe — a single large AI training run can consume as much power as 120 American homes use in an entire year. The existing grid was never designed for this.

Bloom Energy (BE) is building a bridge. Their ceramic fuel cells generate electricity through an electrochemical reaction — continuous, on-site, no combustion, no connection to the traditional grid required. A stack can be installed in 90 days and start generating power immediately, compared to the five-to-ten-year timeline for a new power plant. Nextracker (NXT) takes a very different approach to the same crisis: scale the cheapest source of new electricity ever created. Their motorized mounting systems let fields of solar panels follow the sun across the sky, squeezing up to 25% more energy from the same equipment, and have become standard equipment at the largest solar installations in the world. And Argan (AGX) does something more traditional but increasingly rare: it designs, engineers, and actually builds power plants from the ground up. As demand for gas turbine generation surges to feed AI data centers, Argan's skillset is suddenly in short supply.

But generating power is only half the equation. Someone also has to own the physical sites where those electrons get converted into compute. Three companies in this district were originally built to mine Bitcoin — which required exactly the same infrastructure an AI data center needs: massive power connections, serious cooling systems, and dense racks of processors. It turns out that infrastructure is worth a lot more to an AI customer than to a Bitcoin miner. Hut 8 (HUT) flexes between mining and AI hosting depending on which customer pays more. TeraWulf (WULF) does the same with a distinctive emphasis on clean energy, running primarily on nuclear and hydroelectric power. And Cipher Mining (CIFR) has leaned hardest into the pivot, signing a roughly $5.5 billion 15-year lease with Amazon Web Services to deliver 300 MW of AI capacity in 2026, plus a 168 MW 10-year HPC agreement with Fluidstack worth about $3 billion, plus a 200 MW Ohio site and 95% of a 1-gigawatt West Texas campus. Cipher is transforming into a hybrid Bitcoin miner and power landlord for AI tenants — and is increasingly priced that way.

construction_yards.title: The Construction Yards
construction_yards.subtitle: The contractors who turn empty land into functioning infrastructure
intro: Generating power is still just half the battle. Someone has to build the buildings, pour the foundations, pull the wire, connect the pipes, lay the roads, and keep it all environmentally responsible. When a new data center rises out of empty desert, a small army of specialty contractors shows up first. The Construction Yards is where that army lives.

Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) grades the site and pours the foundations, turning empty land into a functioning campus. Cardinal Infrastructure Group (CDNL) handles the wet utilities — water, sewer, stormwater — and the site work that has to happen before anything vertical can begin. MasTec (MTZ) runs the heavy power lines from the grid to the property line, plus the pipelines and cell towers that connect the larger network. Construction Partners (ROAD) builds the highways that get everyone from the outside world to the facility, because every data center still needs roads to reach it.

Inside the building, Comfort Systems USA (FIX) installs the HVAC, plumbing, and electrical systems while IES Holdings (IESC) handles the specialized high-voltage electrical work — wiring facilities that draw as much power as small towns. Legence (LGN) provides engineering, installation, and maintenance for the mission-critical mechanical systems inside technology, life sciences, healthcare, and education buildings. Applied Digital (APLD) takes it one step further, designing entire AI-native data-center facilities from scratch with electrical and cooling systems engineered for demands traditional buildings were never meant to handle. And CECO Environmental (CECO) installs the scrubbers, filters, and wastewater-treatment equipment that ensure all this growth doesn't come at the expense of the air and water around it. Before a single server rack spins up, these companies have already done most of the real work.

arsenal.title: The Arsenal
arsenal.subtitle: Drones, rockets, satellites, and the parts that defend and extend the city
intro: The city expands beyond Earth. And it needs to be defended. The future of military power is increasingly autonomous, increasingly digital, and increasingly orbital — and the Arsenal is where the drones, rockets, satellites, and precision components that reshape national security and open the space economy get built.

Kratos Defense (KTOS) builds combat drones around a radical idea: make them affordable enough that military forces can deploy them in large numbers and accept losing some. Traditional fighter jets cost tens of millions each, which makes every loss devastating. Kratos's drones cost a fraction of that, fundamentally changing the math of air combat. Karman Holdings (KRMN) provides the precision components inside advanced military platforms — work that requires decades of specialized knowledge and security clearances competitors simply cannot acquire quickly.

In space, Rocket Lab (RKLB) operates one of the most reliable small launch vehicles in the world with its Electron rocket, and the next-generation Neutron rocket — designed to carry 40 times more weight to orbit — targets first flight in mid-2026. York Space Systems (YSS) brings assembly-line thinking to satellite manufacturing, building standardized platforms the way a car factory builds vehicles instead of one-off science projects. Planet Labs (PL) operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, photographing every square meter of the planet every single day, with a 10+ billion km² archive and over $500M of recent deals with Sweden, Japan, Germany, and NATO allies.

Back on Earth, Mercury Systems (MRCY) builds the computing brains inside military radar and missile-defense systems, turning torrents of raw sensor data into actionable intelligence in fractions of a second. And VSE Corporation (VSEC) keeps military and commercial aircraft flying by providing the maintenance, repair, and spare parts complex machines constantly require — every flight hour generates wear, and a grounded aircraft is a mission that can't be flown.

clinic.title: The Clinic
clinic.subtitle: Finding disease before it becomes unbearable
intro: Before there can be treatment, there has to be detection. The idea at the heart of modern medicine is deceptively simple: find disease earlier and everything changes. The Clinic is a small district but a powerful one, and everything it does flows from that one idea.

Guardant Health (GH) pioneered a way to detect and monitor cancer from a simple blood draw. When tumor cells die, they release tiny fragments of their DNA into the bloodstream — Guardant's test captures those fragments, sequences them, and reads the genetic mutations they carry. The result reveals not just that cancer is present, but what specific type it is and which treatments are most likely to work. No surgery, no biopsy needle, just a tube of blood. They can now even screen seemingly healthy people for early-stage colorectal cancer before symptoms appear.

Natera (NTRA) uses the same basic concept — reading DNA fragments floating in blood — for different purposes. During pregnancy, their test can detect genetic conditions in the baby as early as the first trimester. After cancer treatment, their test can find microscopic traces of residual disease months before a traditional scan would catch it, giving doctors the chance to act before the cancer regains a foothold. Adaptive Biotechnologies (ADPT) takes the idea even further: every infection, every vaccine, every cancer a person has ever encountered leaves a unique molecular fingerprint on their immune cells — and Adaptive's technology decodes those fingerprints to detect disease, match treatments, and understand immune response at a level previously impossible. Three companies, one mission: don't wait until the damage is done.

research_labs.title: The Research Labs
research_labs.subtitle: Treating disease by its molecular mechanism — the largest district in the city
intro: The Research Labs is by far the largest district in the city — thirty companies working on some of the most remarkable medical innovation in human history. The unifying thesis is to move beyond treating diseases by where they happen in the body, and start treating them by their molecular mechanism. Some of these companies will face setbacks. Some will deliver breakthroughs that reshape medicine. The breadth of what is happening here is extraordinary.

Start with the giants. Eli Lilly (LLY) is the most valuable healthcare company in the world, riding the GLP-1 obesity wave with Mounjaro and Zepbound while building a deep next-generation pipeline. Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) effectively cured cystic fibrosis and is now expanding into pain (suzetrigine), kidney disease, and gene therapy with Casgevy for sickle cell. Gilead (GILD) built the franchise that turned HIV into a manageable condition and is now pushing into oncology and inflammation. argenx (ARGX) created Vyvgart, the first FcRn-blocker, which has become the new standard of care for myasthenia gravis and is being expanded into a half-dozen autoimmune indications.

The mid-cap rare-disease specialists do extraordinary work. Madrigal (MDGL) developed the first real medication for serious liver disease — a once-daily pill for what used to have only one prescription, 'eat better and exercise more.' BridgeBio (BBIO) hunts rare genetic diseases that individually affect few but collectively touch tens of millions. Insmed (INSM) is rebuilding pulmonary medicine with Arikayce for serious lung infections and brensocatib (TPIP) for bronchiectasis. Tarsus (TARS), Glaukos (GKOS), Rhythm (RYTM), Ascendis (ASND), PTC (PTCT), and Mirum (MIRM) each tackle specific diseases that had no good treatment before — eye mites affecting 25 million Americans, glaucoma, broken hunger signals, daily injections turned weekly, childhood muscle disorders, and bile-processing failures in kids.

In oncology, Celcuity (CELC) and Cogent (COGT) match patients to therapies based on their tumor's molecular fingerprint — stop guessing which cancer drug works, test it on the actual tumor first. Arrowhead (ARWR) silences disease-causing genes before they can do damage, like shredding a bad recipe before the kitchen can use it. Revolution Medicines (RVMD) finally found a way to drug a cancer target considered impossible for decades — a set of mutations found in roughly one out of every three cancers, including many of the deadliest forms. Nuvalent (NUVL) designs precision lung cancer therapies engineered to block the escape routes tumors use to develop resistance. CG Oncology (CGON) built an engineered virus that selectively infects bladder tumors and bursts them from the inside. Immunocore (IMCR) developed the first approved TCR therapy, Kimmtrak, for a rare eye cancer — and is expanding the platform. Terns (TERN) attacks the same liver disease as Madrigal but through a different mechanism, creating combination potential. Abivax (ABVX) is developing what could become the first oral pill for moderate-to-severe inflammatory bowel disease. And TransMedics (TMDX) has revolutionized organ transplantation — keeping donor organs warm and functioning during transport instead of racing them in ice.

The next wave includes companies in registration or late-stage clinical work. NewAmsterdam Pharma (NAMS) is bringing obicetrapib — an oral CETP inhibitor that reduced LDL cholesterol by 33% in a Phase 3 trial — to market in Europe with partner Menarini, with EMA decision expected 2H26. Mineralys (MLYS) is developing lorundrostat for treatment-resistant hypertension, a massive market with no good options. Praxis Precision (PRAX) is in late-stage trials for essential tremor and epilepsy. Forte Biosciences (FBRX) targets autoimmune diseases through a novel anti-CD122 mechanism. Dianthus (DNTH), Protagonist (PTGX), and Nektar (NKTR) round out the late-stage block — claseprubart in Phase 3 CIDP, rusfertide NDA filed for polycythemia vera with Takeda, and rezpegaldesleukin hitting endpoints in atopic dermatitis. Thirty companies, one thesis, and a staggering pipeline of medicine that did not exist a decade ago.

main_street.title: Main Street
main_street.subtitle: Where people live, shop, eat, learn, and have fun — the biggest district in the city
intro: A city is ultimately about people. Main Street is where they actually spend their days — buying things, eating things, traveling, training for jobs, working out, being entertained, and living their lives. It is by far the largest district in the city by company count, because the consumer economy is enormous.

It starts with the megaliths. Apple (AAPL) puts a supercomputer in a billion pockets and sells the services that run on it. Amazon (AMZN) is the everything-store that ships, the cloud that hosts most of the internet, and the advertising platform that monetizes the rest. Costco (COST) is the bulk-buying institution that earned a cult following by treating its customers and its employees better than retail conventionally allows. Tesla (TSLA) is the EV company that became an energy company that became an AI company. Mega names dominate by market cap, but the smaller names tell more interesting stories.

The retail and lifestyle block. Boot Barn (BOOT) outfits the workforce that physically builds America. Deckers (DECK) owns Hoka and Ugg, two cult footwear brands. Ross Stores (ROST) runs the off-price treasure-hunt model. Warby Parker (WRBY) brought eyewear online with $95 prescription glasses. SharkNinja (SN) builds household appliances that punch above their price. Amer Sports (AS) owns Wilson, Salomon, Arc'teryx, and a portfolio of premium outdoor brands.

Food and drink. Celsius (CELH) turned energy drinks healthier and became a cultural phenomenon. CAVA (CAVA) is the Mediterranean Chipotle, scaling toward 1,000 restaurants. Vita Coco (COCO) owns 42% of the U.S. coconut water market. The Chefs' Warehouse (CHEF) distributes specialty ingredients to the country's best restaurants. Mama's Creations (MAMA) makes the deli-prepared meals at your grocery store. Neogen (NEOG) keeps food and animal products safe across the supply chain.

Travel, hospitality, and experiences. Hilton (HLT) operates one of the world's largest hotel networks. Royal Caribbean (RCL) and Viking (VIK) run the cruise industry's two most-loved brands — Royal at scale, Viking at the premium end. Lindblad Expeditions (LIND) runs the polar adventure travel category in partnership with National Geographic. DoorDash (DASH) delivers the food that gets ordered when no one wants to leave. MercadoLibre (MELI) is the Amazon of Latin America, plus a fintech business as big as the marketplace.

Entertainment — the part of Main Street where people go for fun. Netflix (NFLX) defined and now defends the streaming category with over 300 million subscribers worldwide. Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) owns Rockstar Games and 2K Sports, with Grand Theft Auto VI arriving in 2026 as likely the biggest entertainment launch in history. Live Nation (LYV) runs the global concert business through Ticketmaster and its venue network. TKO Group (TKO) houses both UFC and WWE under one roof. IMAX (IMAX) operates the premium large-format theater network. And Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) operates the most talked-about venue in the world — a 17,600-seat immersive arena in Las Vegas with a 16K wraparound LED screen, now the top-grossing venue in the world.

The skilled-trades and wellness block. UTI (Universal Technical Institute) and Lincoln Educational Services (LINC) train the HVAC technicians, diesel mechanics, welders, and electricians the country cannot build data centers without. American Public Education (APEI) runs the largest online university serving the U.S. military and federal workforce. Life Time Holdings (LTH) operates resort-style wellness campuses. Hinge Health (HNGE) treats chronic back and joint pain through a smartphone app and wearable sensors.

Finally, the freight and identity infrastructure of the consumer economy. Saia (SAIA) and XPO (XPO) move the freight that keeps shelves full. Carvana (CVNA) reinvented used car retail. Clear Secure (YOU) runs biometric identity verification at airports with 30+ million members.

software_layer.title: The Software Layer
software_layer.subtitle: The code that runs quietly beneath everything else
intro: Not every software company belongs in a futuristic city. These six do, because they build the scaffolding that lets everything else work — the cloud, the edge, the insurance backbone, the trades management system, and the business travel platform. Unsexy, essential, and increasingly AI-powered.

JFrog (FROG) manages what you might call the supply chain of software — moving code from a developer's laptop through testing and security checks to the servers that run it in production, making sure no malicious code slips in along the way. DigitalOcean (DOCN) is the developer-friendly cloud for startups and small businesses that can't afford the complexity of AWS; its AI-native revenue has been doubling for five straight quarters as AI startups look for a simpler home. Fastly (FSLY) runs a programmable edge network that speeds up the internet and stops attacks, concentrating servers in the most network-dense hubs rather than spreading them everywhere.

Guidewire (GWRE) is the operating system of the property and casualty insurance industry — more than 570 insurers in 42 countries, including Allianz, AXA, and Liberty Mutual, run their core claims, policy, and billing systems on it. The shift from on-premise to Guidewire Cloud is a multi-year migration that is now hitting full stride. ServiceTitan (TTAN) is doing for the skilled trades what Guidewire did for insurance — plumbers, electricians, HVAC contractors, landscapers — a full business management suite that lets a family-owned contractor compete with a national chain. And Navan (NAVN) is rebuilding corporate travel and expense management around a traveler-first experience, with $7.6B in annual gross bookings across 10,000+ business customers.

financial_district.title: The Financial District
financial_district.subtitle: Where the money moves and the deals get done
intro: None of this gets built without capital. The Financial District is where the money for the rest of the city flows — the investment banks, the exchanges, and a few specialists where finance meets the physical world in interesting ways.

Evercore (EVR) is one of the most respected independent investment banks in the world — the financial architect behind some of the most consequential M&A deals and IPOs in the economy. Piper Sandler (PIPR) does similar work with particular depth in healthcare and technology, helping innovative smaller companies raise the capital they need to grow from promising startup to established player. MIAX runs electronic options exchanges — the marketplaces where a farmer locks in next year's crop price and a money manager buys insurance against a market drop.

FTAI Aviation (FTAI) occupies a fascinating niche where finance meets aerospace — leasing commercial jet engines to airlines and, crucially, rebuilding them in-house through a process that requires years of specialized knowledge and is extremely hard for newcomers to replicate. And Mirion Technologies (MIR) makes the instruments that detect and measure radiation — the monitors worn by nuclear-plant workers, the calibration devices inside hospital imaging equipment, the environmental sensors around radioactive materials. As interest in nuclear energy surges to power AI data centers, Mirion's safety technology only becomes more critical.

AMKR.name: Amkor Technology
AMKR.big_picture: Packages finished chips into protective housings — the final step before silicon meets the real world.
AMKR.story: Amkor Technology handles the final step of chipmaking: taking a finished chip — a delicate flake of silicon thinner than a fingernail — and encasing it in a protective housing with hundreds of tiny electrical connections, so it can survive the intense heat and vibration inside a working computer. Advanced packaging has become a bottleneck in AI chip production, and Amkor is one of the few companies with the technology to do it at scale.
AMKR.sound_bite: Every chip needs armor before it hits the real world. Amkor is the armorer.

NVMI.name: Nova
NVMI.big_picture: Inspection tools that photograph microscopic chip layers with beams of light and electrons.
NVMI.story: Modern chips have features measured in billionths of a meter — structures so small that a single virus would tower over them like a skyscraper. Building something at that scale requires extraordinary precision at every step. Nova builds the inspection tools that chipmakers use to verify each microscopic layer during production, essentially photographing the chip's surface with beams of light and electrons to catch defects invisible to any human eye.
NVMI.sound_bite: The microscope that saves a billion-dollar fab from one bad layer.

MKSI.name: MKS Instruments
MKSI.big_picture: Controls gases, temperatures, and energy inside chip fab chambers — perfect weather inside a sealed room.
MKSI.story: MKS Instruments provides the instruments that control the gases, temperatures, and energy inside chip fabrication chambers. Think of it as maintaining perfect weather conditions inside a sealed room where even a single stray particle could ruin everything. Every major semiconductor fab in the world relies on MKS equipment.
MKSI.sound_bite: Perfect weather inside a room where one stray particle ruins everything.

LSCC.name: Lattice Semiconductor
LSCC.big_picture: Programmable chips that can be reconfigured after installation — like a toolbox that transforms into whatever tool you need.
LSCC.story: Lattice Semiconductor makes programmable chips — processors that can be reconfigured after installation, like a toolbox that can transform into whatever tool you need at the moment. Traditional chips are designed to do one job forever. Lattice's chips can be reprogrammed to handle new tasks as requirements change, making them invaluable in factories, cars, and communication equipment where flexibility matters.
LSCC.sound_bite: A chip that changes its mind.

RMBS.name: Rambus
RMBS.big_picture: Designs the rules that govern how processors and memory chips talk — determines how fast the whole computer runs.
RMBS.story: Rambus designs the rules that govern how processors and memory chips communicate. Think of memory like a library and the processor like a reader — Rambus designs the system that determines how fast the reader can check out and return books. The faster that exchange happens, the faster the entire computer runs, which is why Rambus's memory-interface IP sits inside nearly every modern data center.
RMBS.sound_bite: The librarian that decides how fast the computer can think.

MP.name: MP Materials
MP.big_picture: Only large-scale rare-earth producer in the Western Hemisphere — magnets for EVs, wind, fighter jets.
MP.story: MP Materials mines and processes rare-earth elements — the minerals that create the powerful permanent magnets inside electric car motors, wind turbines, fighter jets, and computer hard drives. These elements have unique magnetic properties no other material can replicate, which is why they're called 'rare' — not because they're scarce in the earth's crust, but because extracting and refining them is extraordinarily difficult. MP is the only large-scale rare-earth producer in the Western Hemisphere.
MP.sound_bite: The only rare-earth supplier on this side of the Pacific.

CRDO.name: Credo Technology
CRDO.big_picture: High-speed cables and connection chips that carry signals between AI processors — the nervous system of AI.
CRDO.story: Credo Technology builds the high-speed cables and connection chips that carry signals between processors at billions of cycles per second. Picture a room full of brilliant people trying to solve a problem together, but they can only communicate by passing notes — the speed of those notes determines how fast the group can think. Credo makes the notes move at light speed. They are the nervous system of artificial intelligence, and without them, even the most powerful processors sit idle waiting for information.
CRDO.sound_bite: Even the fastest AI chip is useless without Credo's wiring.

ALAB.name: Astera Labs
ALAB.big_picture: Intelligent traffic controllers for data flowing between AI processors stacked in a single rack.
ALAB.story: Astera Labs solves data movement at the rack level. When hundreds of processors are stacked together in a single cabinet, they generate an enormous amount of data traffic — imagine thousands of cars all trying to merge onto the same highway at once. Astera builds the intelligent traffic controllers that manage those data flows, prioritizing the most urgent signals, preventing collisions, and making sure nothing gets stuck in a bottleneck. Their technology has become essential inside the world's largest AI installations.
ALAB.sound_bite: The traffic cops inside every AI rack.

MTSI.name: MACOM Technology
MTSI.big_picture: Chips that convert electrical signals into laser light and back — the magic behind fiber-optic networks.
MTSI.story: MACOM Technology designs the chips that make fiber-optic networks work. On one end, they take an electrical signal — the language computers speak — and use a tiny laser to convert it into a beam of light that shoots through the glass fiber. On the other end, a sensor catches that light and converts it back into an electrical signal the receiving computer can understand. This conversion happens billions of times per second.
MTSI.sound_bite: Turning electricity into light and back, billions of times per second.

SITM.name: SiTime
SITM.big_picture: Precision silicon clocks — the tiny heartbeats that keep electronic signals synchronized.
SITM.story: SiTime makes something deceptively simple but absolutely essential: precision timing devices. Every electronic system needs a clock — a tiny heartbeat that keeps all its signals perfectly synchronized, like a conductor keeping an orchestra in time. If the timing drifts even a fraction of a second, signals collide and data gets corrupted. SiTime's silicon-based clocks are smaller, more accurate, and more resilient to vibration and temperature changes than traditional alternatives, which is why they're showing up inside everything from AI data centers to self-driving cars.
SITM.sound_bite: The conductor's baton inside every electronic orchestra.

SMTC.name: Semtech
SMTC.big_picture: Chips that clean up degraded electronic signals — quality control for data moving through cables and circuits.
SMTC.story: Semtech acts as the quality-control system for electronic signals. As data travels through cables and circuits, the signal gradually degrades — like a photocopy of a photocopy getting fuzzier each time. Semtech's chips clean up and restore those signals so they arrive crisp and intact, even after traveling long distances. Their technology sits inside data-center switches, smart-city sensors, and agricultural monitoring systems around the world.
SMTC.sound_bite: Stops the photocopy-of-a-photocopy problem inside every cable.

ALGM.name: Allegro MicroSystems
ALGM.big_picture: Sensors that detect motion, position, and electrical current inside EVs, factory robots, and data center cooling.
ALGM.story: Allegro MicroSystems builds sensors that detect motion, position, and electrical current inside machines. When an electric car's motor spins, Allegro's sensors measure exactly how fast and in what direction, allowing the control system to adjust power delivery thousands of times per second. The same principle applies to factory robots, data-center cooling fans, and any application where something moves and needs to be precisely controlled.
ALGM.sound_bite: If it spins, Allegro is watching.

BE.name: Bloom Energy
BE.big_picture: On-site fuel cells that generate power in 90 days vs. 5-10 years for a new power plant — the answer to AI's electricity crisis.
BE.story: Bloom Energy's fuel cells generate electricity through an electrochemical reaction — similar in concept to a battery, but instead of storing energy and running down, they continuously convert natural gas or hydrogen into electricity as long as fuel is supplied. No combustion, no turbines, no connection to the traditional power grid required. A stack of Bloom's ceramic fuel cells can be installed on-site in as little as 90 days and start generating power immediately. Compare that to the five-to-ten-year timeline for building a new power plant, and you start to understand why some of the world's largest investors are pouring billions into deploying them at AI data centers. Bloom is doubling its manufacturing capacity to meet the surge in demand.
BE.sound_bite: 90 days to power a data center, not 10 years.

NXT.name: Nextracker
NXT.big_picture: Motorized trackers that let solar panels follow the sun — squeezing 25% more energy from the same equipment.
NXT.story: Nextracker makes the motorized mounting systems that allow massive fields of solar panels to follow the sun across the sky throughout the day, like sunflowers turning toward light. A fixed solar panel captures whatever light hits it at the angle it happens to be facing. A tracking panel adjusts its angle every few minutes to face the sun directly, squeezing up to 25% more energy from the same equipment. It has become standard equipment at the largest solar installations in the world.
NXT.sound_bite: Solar panels that follow the sun like sunflowers.

FIX.name: Comfort Systems USA
FIX.big_picture: Installs the HVAC, plumbing, and electrical guts of every new data center and hospital being built.
FIX.story: Comfort Systems USA installs the heating, cooling, plumbing, electrical, and fire-protection systems inside large commercial buildings. When a new data center or hospital is constructed, Comfort Systems is the company making sure the air flows properly, the water runs, the temperature stays within precise limits, and the power reaches every room. They've become one of the most in-demand contractors in the country.
FIX.sound_bite: Every data center America builds, Comfort Systems plumbs and wires.

IESC.name: IES Holdings
IESC.big_picture: Specialized heavy-duty electrical contracting for facilities that draw as much power as a small town.
IESC.story: IES Holdings handles the specialized electrical work — running the heavy-duty wiring that connects buildings to power sources and distributing electricity safely through facilities that may draw as much power as a small town.
IESC.sound_bite: When a data center needs as much power as a small town, IES wires it.

STRL.name: Sterling Infrastructure
STRL.big_picture: Massive earthwork that turns empty land into functioning data center campuses.
STRL.story: Sterling Infrastructure does the massive earthwork that turns empty land into a functioning data-center campus — grading the site, pouring foundations, installing underground water and electrical systems, and building the roads within the campus.
STRL.sound_bite: Before a data center can compute, Sterling has to move the dirt.

MTZ.name: MasTec
MTZ.big_picture: One of the country's largest infrastructure contractors — power lines, pipelines, cell towers.
MTZ.story: MasTec is one of the largest infrastructure contractors in the country, building the power lines that carry electricity from generation sources to where it's needed, the pipelines that deliver fuel, and the cell towers that connect wireless networks.
MTZ.sound_bite: If electricity, fuel, or wireless signal has to travel, MasTec probably built the path.

ROAD.name: Construction Partners
ROAD.big_picture: Builds and maintains the highways connecting every data center, power plant, and factory to the outside world.
ROAD.story: Construction Partners builds and maintains the highways connecting all of these facilities to the outside world — easy to overlook, but every data center, every power plant, every factory needs roads to reach it.
ROAD.sound_bite: You can't run a data center no one can drive to.

APLD.name: Applied Digital
APLD.big_picture: Building AI-native data center facilities from scratch, engineered for extreme electrical and cooling demands.
APLD.story: Applied Digital is building entire data-center facilities designed from scratch for AI computing, with electrical and cooling systems engineered specifically for the extreme demands that traditional buildings were never meant to handle.
APLD.sound_bite: Traditional data centers were built for websites. Applied Digital is building for AI.

LGN.name: Legence
LGN.big_picture: Engineering, installation, and maintenance for mission-critical building systems in tech, life sciences, healthcare, and education.
LGN.story: Legence is a leading provider of engineering, installation, and maintenance services for mission-critical systems inside technically demanding buildings — data centers, semiconductor fabs, biotech labs, hospitals, and university research facilities. Founded in January 2025 and headquartered in San Jose, Legence focuses on the high-growth sectors where building systems absolutely cannot fail. When a chip fab needs mechanical engineering that meets class-10 cleanroom standards, or a biotech lab needs HVAC that maintains exact pressure gradients between rooms, Legence is the company that designs, installs, and services it.
LGN.sound_bite: The specialist who handles the mechanical systems nobody else knows how to service.

CDNL.name: Cardinal Infrastructure Group
CDNL.big_picture: Wet utilities, site development, and civil contracting across the southeastern US.
CDNL.story: Cardinal Infrastructure Group is a civil contracting company that provides site development and infrastructure services to residential, commercial, industrial, municipal, and state infrastructure markets in the southeastern United States. They handle wet utility installations of water, sewer, and stormwater systems, as well as grading, site clearing, erosion control, drilling and blasting, paving, and related site services. Before anything vertical can be built on a piece of land, Cardinal has to handle what happens underneath it.
CDNL.sound_bite: The work that happens before anything vertical can begin.

CECO.name: CECO Environmental
CECO.big_picture: Scrubbers, filters, and wastewater treatment that keep industrial growth from trashing the air and water.
CECO.story: CECO Environmental designs and builds the systems that keep industrial operations environmentally responsible — the scrubbers that remove pollutants from factory exhaust, the filters that clean industrial air, and the treatment equipment that purifies wastewater before it returns to rivers and aquifers. It's unglamorous work that ensures economic growth doesn't come at the expense of the air we breathe and the water we drink.
CECO.sound_bite: Growth without pollution. CECO is how that happens.

CRS.name: Carpenter Technology
CRS.big_picture: Forges specialty metals that withstand 2,000° heat, corrosive chemicals, and extreme pressure.
CRS.story: Carpenter Technology forges specialty metals and advanced alloys — materials engineered at the atomic level to withstand 2,000-degree temperatures, corrosive chemicals, and thousands of pounds of pressure. These go into jet-engine turbine blades, surgical implants, and nuclear reactor components — applications where a material failure isn't an inconvenience but a catastrophe.
CRS.sound_bite: Metals so specialized that failure isn't an inconvenience. It's a catastrophe.

RBC.name: RBC Bearings
RBC.big_picture: Precision bearings for helicopter rotors, submarines, and industrial machinery — flawless under extreme force.
RBC.story: RBC Bearings makes the precision bearings that enable smooth rotation inside helicopter rotors, submarine propulsion systems, and industrial machinery. A bearing is a deceptively simple concept — it reduces friction between moving parts — but engineering one that operates flawlessly under thousands of pounds of force, at extreme temperatures, for years without maintenance, is anything but simple.
RBC.sound_bite: A simple idea, engineered to survive where nothing else can.

SANM.name: Sanmina
SANM.big_picture: Contract manufacturer of complex electronics — medical imaging, military vehicles, cloud servers.
SANM.story: Sanmina is one of the world's most important contract manufacturers — the company brilliant inventors turn to when they've designed something remarkable but need someone who can actually build it. Sanmina handles complex electronic assemblies: the densely packed circuit boards inside medical imaging machines, the communication systems inside military vehicles, the server boards that run cloud computing.
SANM.sound_bite: The hidden hands that turn brilliant designs into actual products.

FN.name: Fabrinet
FN.big_picture: Precision contract manufacturing for optics aligned to tolerances thinner than a wavelength of light.
FN.story: Fabrinet specializes in work that requires almost unimaginable precision — the tiny optical components inside fiber-optic networks that must be aligned to tolerances measured in millionths of a meter, thinner than a wavelength of light. They are one of the hidden hands that turn designs into reality.
FN.sound_bite: Precision so extreme you measure it in wavelengths of light.

WWD.name: Woodward
WWD.big_picture: Control systems that precisely meter fuel inside aircraft engines and industrial turbines.
WWD.story: Woodward designs the control systems that regulate fuel flow and power output inside aircraft engines and industrial turbines. When a pilot pushes the throttle forward, Woodward's technology is what precisely meters the fuel, adjusts the air intake, and ensures the engine delivers exactly the right amount of power without overheating or flaming out.
WWD.sound_bite: Pushing the throttle is easy. Surviving what happens next is Woodward.

CR.name: Crane Company
CR.big_picture: Engineered valves, sensors, and fluid-handling systems for aircraft, naval vessels, and factories.
CR.story: Crane Company supplies the engineered valves, sensors, and fluid-handling systems used in aircraft, naval vessels, and factories — components that control the flow of fuel, hydraulic fluid, and air in environments where reliability is everything.
CR.sound_bite: The valves and sensors that absolutely cannot fail because lives depend on them.

LFUS.name: Littelfuse
LFUS.big_picture: Circuit protection components that sacrifice themselves to save expensive equipment during surges.
LFUS.story: Littelfuse protects electrical circuits from destruction. Every time there's a power surge, a lightning strike, or an unexpected spike in current, Littelfuse's components sacrifice themselves — absorbing the excess energy and breaking the circuit before expensive equipment is damaged, like a fuse blowing to protect your house's wiring. As the world fills with more sensitive electronics, that protection becomes more important, not less.
LFUS.sound_bite: Dies so the rest of the circuit can live.

AGX.name: Argan
AGX.big_picture: Designs, engineers, and constructs power plants from the ground up.
AGX.story: Argan builds power plants from the ground up — designing, engineering, and constructing the generation facilities that create the electricity the rest of the city depends on. As gas turbine demand surges to power AI data centers, Argan's skillset is in rare supply.
AGX.sound_bite: Someone has to actually build the power plants. Argan is that someone.

TTMI.name: TTM Technologies
TTMI.big_picture: Top-five global PCB manufacturer, largest supplier of PCBs to the U.S. military, fastest growth from AI accelerators.
TTMI.story: TTM Technologies is one of the top five printed circuit board manufacturers in the world, the largest in North America, and the largest supplier of PCBs to the U.S. military. Aerospace and defense account for ~45% of revenue, supported by a record $1.6B backlog and a recent $200M multi-year Raytheon contract for LTAMDS radar components. But the fastest-growing part of the business is data center computing — combined with networking, it now represents 36% of total revenue, up from the mid-20s just a year ago, driven by demand for high-density PCBs used in AI accelerators. A new advanced-technology facility in Syracuse is ramping in the second half of 2026.
TTMI.sound_bite: Defense backlog for the floor, AI accelerators for the growth.

MOD.name: Modine Manufacturing
MOD.big_picture: Liquid cooling for AI data centers — transformed from a legacy auto-parts company into an AI infrastructure name.
MOD.story: A single rack of AI processors generates as much warmth as a small house, and a data center full of them produces heat like a furnace. Traditional air conditioning simply cannot keep up. Modine Manufacturing designs advanced cooling systems that pump liquid directly past the hottest components, absorbing heat the way a car's radiator cools an engine, then carrying it away to be dissipated safely. They've transformed from a legacy auto-parts company into one of the most important names in AI infrastructure.
MOD.sound_bite: The radiator company that woke up one morning as an AI infrastructure play.

BELFB.name: Bel Fuse
BELFB.big_picture: Power supplies and connectivity components hiding inside networking gear, radar, and industrial controls.
BELFB.story: Bel Fuse manufactures the electronic power supplies and connectivity components found inside an enormous range of essential equipment. Networking gear, military radar systems, industrial controls — they all need reliable power conversion and data connections, and Bel Fuse is one of those small companies whose parts quietly show up everywhere.
BELFB.sound_bite: The parts you'll never see but that everything depends on.

EVR.name: Evercore
EVR.big_picture: Independent investment bank — the financial architects behind the economy's biggest M&A deals and IPOs.
EVR.story: Evercore is one of the most respected independent investment banks in the world. When two large companies are considering merging, or a fast-growing private company is ready to sell shares to the public for the first time, Evercore advises on how to structure the deal, what price is fair, and how to navigate the complex negotiations involved.
EVR.sound_bite: When billion-dollar deals happen, Evercore is usually in the room.

PIPR.name: Piper Sandler Companies
PIPR.big_picture: Investment bank with deep healthcare and tech expertise — helps innovative smaller companies raise capital.
PIPR.story: Piper Sandler does deal-advisory work with particular expertise in healthcare and technology, often helping innovative smaller companies raise the capital they need to grow from promising startup to established player.
PIPR.sound_bite: The boutique investment bank for the next wave of healthcare and tech.

MIAX.name: Miami International Holdings
MIAX.big_picture: Runs options exchanges — the marketplaces where investors buy insurance against losses or bets on price moves.
MIAX.story: MIAX runs options exchanges — the electronic marketplaces where investors buy and sell contracts that let them protect against losses or profit from price changes. Think of it like insurance for investments: a farmer might lock in a price for next year's harvest, and a money manager might protect against a market downturn, and both transactions happen on an exchange like MIAX.
MIAX.sound_bite: Every financial 'just in case' has to trade somewhere. MIAX is one of those somewheres.

FTAI.name: FTAI Aviation
FTAI.big_picture: Leases and rebuilds commercial jet engines — a finance-meets-aerospace business with a deep repair moat.
FTAI.story: FTAI Aviation occupies a fascinating niche where finance meets aerospace. They lease commercial jet engines to airlines and, crucially, have built deep in-house expertise in overhauling and repairing them. When an engine comes in for service, their technicians disassemble it, inspect every component, replace what's worn, and return it to like-new condition — a process that requires years of specialized knowledge and is extremely hard for newcomers to replicate.
FTAI.sound_bite: A jet engine rebuilder with a moat built out of decades of know-how.

MIR.name: Mirion Technologies
MIR.big_picture: Radiation detection instruments — critical as nuclear energy comes back to power AI data centers.
MIR.story: Mirion Technologies provides the instruments that detect and measure radiation — the monitors worn by workers in nuclear power plants, the calibration devices for hospital imaging equipment, and the environmental sensors that ensure radioactive materials are handled safely. As interest in nuclear energy surges, particularly for powering AI data centers, this kind of safety technology becomes even more critical.
MIR.sound_bite: If nuclear is coming back for AI, so is Mirion.

KTOS.name: Kratos Defense
KTOS.big_picture: Affordable combat drones — changes the math of air combat by being cheap enough to lose.
KTOS.story: Kratos Defense builds drone systems designed around a radical idea: make them affordable enough that military forces can deploy them in large numbers and accept losing some. Traditional fighter jets cost tens of millions of dollars each, making every loss devastating. Kratos's drones cost a fraction of that, fundamentally changing the math of air combat.
KTOS.sound_bite: Cheap enough to lose. That's the whole point.

KRMN.name: Karman Holdings
KRMN.big_picture: Precision-engineered components for advanced military platforms — decades of specialized know-how and security clearances.
KRMN.story: Karman Holdings provides the precision-engineered components that go into advanced military platforms — the kind of work that requires decades of specialized knowledge and security clearances that create very high barriers to competition.
KRMN.sound_bite: The moat isn't technology. It's decades of clearances and know-how.

RKLB.name: Rocket Lab
RKLB.big_picture: Electron is one of the most reliable small launch vehicles in the world — Neutron rocket targets 40x payload in 2026.
RKLB.story: Rocket Lab is proving that getting to orbit doesn't have to be the exclusive domain of giant contractors. Their Electron rocket has become one of the most reliable launch vehicles in the world, carrying satellites to orbit with a track record that keeps improving. They recently won their largest contract ever to build a constellation of missile-warning satellites, and their next-generation Neutron rocket — designed to carry 40 times more weight to orbit — is targeting its first flight in mid-2026. If it works, it opens up an enormous new market.
RKLB.sound_bite: The only serious Western alternative to SpaceX for small and medium launch.

YSS.name: York Space Systems
YSS.big_picture: Brings assembly-line thinking to satellites — standardized platforms built like cars, not one-of-a-kind projects.
YSS.story: York Space Systems brings assembly-line thinking to satellite manufacturing. Instead of building each satellite as a one-of-a-kind custom project that takes years, York builds standardized platforms the way a car factory builds vehicles — faster, cheaper, and in much greater quantities. The military needs hundreds of small satellites for communications and early-warning systems, and York's approach makes that affordable for the first time.
YSS.sound_bite: Satellites built on an assembly line. Finally.

PL.name: Planet Labs
PL.big_picture: World's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites — photographing the entire planet every day.
PL.story: Planet Labs operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, with the explicit mission of photographing every square meter of the planet every single day. Their PlanetScope 'Dove' constellation delivers 3-5 meter resolution imagery, their SkySat constellation provides sub-meter resolution and HD video, and their new Pelican constellation is designed for up to 10 revisits per day globally. The archive now exceeds 10 billion km² of imagery going back to 2009. Recent deals with Sweden, Japan, Germany, and NATO allies total over $500 million, cementing Planet's role as a key partner for Western defense and intelligence. A partnership with Nvidia is enabling on-orbit AI inference directly on Pelican satellites.
PL.sound_bite: Photograph the entire planet, every day, and sell the data.

MRCY.name: Mercury Systems
MRCY.big_picture: Computing brains inside military radar — turns raw sensor torrents into actionable intelligence in fractions of a second.
MRCY.story: Mercury Systems builds the computing brains inside military radar and missile-defense systems. When a radar station scans the sky, it generates a torrent of data that must be processed in real time to distinguish a flock of birds from an incoming threat. Mercury builds the hardware that does that processing, turning raw radar signals into actionable intelligence in fractions of a second.
MRCY.sound_bite: The computer that decides, in milliseconds, whether it's a goose or a missile.

VSEC.name: VSE Corporation
VSEC.big_picture: Keeps military and commercial aircraft flying — maintenance, repair, and spare parts for complex machines.
VSEC.story: VSE Corporation keeps military and commercial aircraft flying by providing the maintenance, repair, and spare parts that complex machines constantly require. Every flight hour generates wear that must be addressed, every aging component must be replaced on schedule, and every grounded aircraft represents a mission that can't be flown.
VSEC.sound_bite: A grounded aircraft is a failed mission. VSE keeps them in the air.

GH.name: Guardant Health
GH.big_picture: Detects and monitors cancer from a simple blood draw — no biopsy needle required.
GH.story: Guardant Health has pioneered a way to detect and monitor cancer from a simple blood draw. When tumor cells die, they release tiny fragments of their DNA into the bloodstream. Guardant's test captures those fragments, sequences them, and reads the genetic mutations they carry — revealing not just that cancer is present, but what specific type it is and which treatments are most likely to work. No surgery, no biopsy needle, just a tube of blood. They can now even screen seemingly healthy people for early-stage colorectal cancer before symptoms appear.
GH.sound_bite: Find cancer in a tube of blood, before you ever feel a symptom.

NTRA.name: Natera
NTRA.big_picture: Reads DNA fragments in the blood to detect fetal conditions in pregnancy and microscopic cancer residue after treatment.
NTRA.story: Natera uses the same basic concept as Guardant — reading DNA fragments floating in the blood — for different purposes. During pregnancy, their test can detect genetic conditions in the baby as early as the first trimester. After cancer treatment, their test can find microscopic traces of remaining disease months before a traditional scan would catch it, giving doctors the chance to act before the cancer regains a foothold.
NTRA.sound_bite: Sees cancer coming back months before any scan would.

ADPT.name: Adaptive Biotechnologies
ADPT.big_picture: Reads the immune system like a book — every infection, cancer, and vaccine leaves a molecular fingerprint they can decode.
ADPT.story: Adaptive Biotechnologies reads the immune system like a book — every infection, every cancer, every vaccine a person has ever encountered leaves a unique molecular fingerprint on their immune cells, and Adaptive's technology can decode those fingerprints to detect disease, match treatments, and understand immune response at a level previously impossible.
ADPT.sound_bite: Your immune system is a history book of every disease you've ever fought. Adaptive reads it.

MDGL.name: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals
MDGL.big_picture: First and only approved medication for a serious liver condition affecting tens of millions — a once-daily pill.
MDGL.story: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals developed the first and only approved medication for a serious liver condition that affects tens of millions of Americans. The disease causes fat to accumulate in the liver, which triggers inflammation and progressive scarring. Over time, the scarring can become so severe that the liver fails entirely. Before Madrigal's drug, the only medical advice was to eat better and exercise more. Now there's a once-daily pill that directly addresses the underlying liver damage, with the vast majority of patients who could benefit still untreated.
MDGL.sound_bite: The first real drug for a disease that used to have only one prescription: eat better.

BBIO.name: BridgeBio Pharma
BBIO.big_picture: Hunts rare genetic diseases — first drug for a heart condition where abnormal protein builds up like plaque in a pipe.
BBIO.story: BridgeBio Pharma hunts for rare genetic diseases — conditions that individually affect small numbers of people but collectively touch tens of millions worldwide. Their first major drug treats a heart condition where an abnormal protein gradually builds up in the heart muscle like plaque in a pipe, stiffening it until it can no longer pump effectively. They then announced successful trials for limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, a devastating disease where muscles in the hips and shoulders progressively waste away, and for a rare disorder where the body can't properly regulate calcium levels. Both therapies are heading toward approval.
BBIO.sound_bite: For families with rare genetic diseases, BridgeBio is genuine hope.

PTCT.name: PTC Therapeutics
PTCT.big_picture: Helps the body read past genetic 'typos' in rare childhood muscle diseases and produce functional protein anyway.
PTCT.story: PTC Therapeutics works on rare childhood diseases where the genetic instructions for building muscle protein contain an error, like a typo in a recipe, causing muscles to gradually weaken over time. Their therapies aim to help the body read past that error and produce functional protein anyway.
PTCT.sound_bite: Teaching the body to read past a typo in its own recipe book.

MIRM.name: Mirum Pharmaceuticals
MIRM.big_picture: Treats children whose livers can't process bile — a devastating cause of itching, malnutrition, and organ failure.
MIRM.story: Mirum Pharmaceuticals treats children whose livers can't properly process bile, a digestive fluid that backs up and causes agonizing itching, malnutrition, and potential organ failure.
MIRM.sound_bite: For kids living with a broken liver, Mirum is a lifeline.

RYTM.name: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals
RYTM.big_picture: Restores the brain's hunger-signaling system in rare genetic forms of extreme obesity — not willpower, broken biology.
RYTM.story: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals addresses rare genetic forms of extreme obesity caused by a mutation that breaks the brain's hunger-signaling system. Imagine never feeling full, no matter how much you eat — not a willpower problem, but a broken biological thermostat. Rhythm's medication restores that signal.
RYTM.sound_bite: For patients whose 'full' signal is permanently broken, Rhythm fixes the thermostat.

TARS.name: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals
TARS.big_picture: First approved treatment for an eye condition caused by microscopic mites on eyelashes — 25M Americans affected.
TARS.story: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals created the first approved treatment for a surprisingly common eye condition caused by microscopic mites that live on eyelashes and cause chronic redness and irritation in an estimated 25 million Americans who previously had no effective remedy.
TARS.sound_bite: 25 million Americans have it. Until recently, nobody had a treatment.

GKOS.name: Glaukos
GKOS.big_picture: Tiny implants that relieve glaucoma pressure during routine eye surgery — replacing daily eye drops forever.
GKOS.story: Glaukos has developed tiny implants — so small they're barely visible — that can be inserted during routine eye surgery to relieve the pressure that causes glaucoma, the leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, replacing the burden of daily eye drops with a lasting solution.
GKOS.sound_bite: No more daily eye drops. Just an implant too small to see.

CELC.name: Celcuity
CELC.big_picture: Tests a patient's living cancer cells in the lab to match them to the therapy most likely to work.
CELC.story: Celcuity takes a different approach to cancer. Rather than treating every tumor the same way, they test a patient's living cancer cells in the lab to determine exactly which biological signals are driving the tumor's growth, then match the patient to the therapy most likely to shut those signals down.
CELC.sound_bite: Stop guessing which cancer drug works. Test it on the actual tumor first.

COGT.name: Cogent Biosciences
COGT.big_picture: Designs a cancer drug that fits a specific genetic mutation like a key fits a lock.
COGT.story: Cogent Biosciences targets a specific type of gastrointestinal tumor driven by a known genetic mutation, designing a drug that fits the mutation like a key fits a lock. This represents the broader revolution in cancer care: treating the disease based on its molecular fingerprint rather than simply where it appears in the body.
COGT.sound_bite: A drug designed to fit one mutation, the way a key fits one lock.

ARWR.name: Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals
ARWR.big_picture: Silences disease-causing genes before they can do damage — shredding bad recipes before the body can use them.
ARWR.story: Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals is pioneering an approach that silences disease-causing genes before they can do damage. Many serious illnesses are caused by the body producing too much of a harmful protein — Arrowhead's therapies intercept the genetic instructions for that protein and destroy them, like shredding a bad recipe before the kitchen can use it.
ARWR.sound_bite: Stop the disease before the body reads the recipe.

RVMD.name: Revolution Medicines
RVMD.big_picture: Going after a cancer mutation in 1 of every 3 cancers that was considered 'undruggable' for decades.
RVMD.story: Revolution Medicines is going after one of the most important targets in all of cancer research: a group of mutations found in roughly one out of every three cancers, including many of the deadliest forms. For decades, scientists considered these mutations impossible to treat because the protein's surface was perfectly smooth, with no crevice for a drug molecule to grab onto. Revolution found a way in, and the research community is watching with intense interest.
RVMD.sound_bite: The 'undruggable' target that finally has a door.

NUVL.name: Nuvalent
NUVL.big_picture: Precision lung cancer drugs engineered to block the escape routes tumors use to develop resistance.
NUVL.story: Nuvalent designs precision lung cancer therapies engineered to solve a specific problem: drug resistance. Many targeted cancer drugs work brilliantly at first, but the tumor eventually evolves a workaround and starts growing again. Nuvalent's drugs are designed from the start to block those escape routes, keeping the treatment effective longer.
NUVL.sound_bite: Designed from day one to stay ahead of the tumor's next move.

TERN.name: Terns Pharmaceuticals
TERN.big_picture: Attacks the same liver disease as Madrigal but through a different mechanism — combination potential.
TERN.story: Terns Pharmaceuticals attacks the same serious liver disease as Madrigal but through a different scientific mechanism, which matters because different patients respond to different approaches and combining treatments may prove more powerful than any single drug.
TERN.sound_bite: Different mechanism, same disease. Potential combination partner to Madrigal.

CGON.name: CG Oncology
CGON.big_picture: Engineered a virus that selectively infects bladder tumor cells, replicates until they burst, then spreads to neighboring cancer.
CGON.story: CG Oncology has developed a creative approach to bladder cancer: they've engineered a virus that selectively infects tumor cells, replicates inside them until they burst, and then spreads to neighboring cancer cells — all while leaving healthy tissue untouched. For patients facing the prospect of having their bladder surgically removed, this could be a life-changing alternative.
CGON.sound_bite: A virus that hunts cancer and leaves healthy tissue alone.

ABVX.name: Abivax
ABVX.big_picture: Developing what could become the first oral pill for moderate-to-severe inflammatory bowel disease.
ABVX.story: Abivax is developing what could become the first pill for moderate-to-severe inflammatory bowel disease — a chronic condition where the immune system mistakenly attacks the digestive tract, causing debilitating pain, bleeding, and long-term damage. Current treatments for serious cases require injections or hours-long infusions in a clinic. An effective oral pill would transform daily life for millions of patients.
ABVX.sound_bite: The first pill for a disease that today requires hours in an infusion chair.

TMDX.name: TransMedics
TMDX.big_picture: Keeps donor organs alive and functioning during transport instead of packing them in ice — more organs, more lives saved.
TMDX.story: TransMedics has revolutionized organ transplantation. Traditionally, a donor organ is packed in ice and raced to the recipient in a desperate sprint against time, with the organ deteriorating every minute. TransMedics built a portable system that keeps the organ warm, pumps blood through it, and maintains it in a living, functioning state during the entire journey. This extends how far an organ can travel, allows doctors to evaluate its quality before implanting it, and has dramatically expanded the number of transplants that can be performed.
TMDX.sound_bite: Organs arriving alive instead of on ice. More transplants, more lives.

ASND.name: Ascendis Pharma
ASND.big_picture: Turns daily-injection drugs into weekly or monthly treatments by wrapping them in a slow-release molecular coating.
ASND.story: Ascendis Pharma has solved a practical problem that makes an enormous difference in patients' lives: turning medications that require painful daily injections into formulations that work with just one shot per week, by wrapping the active drug in a molecular coating that slowly releases it over time. For a child facing years of daily growth hormone injections, the difference between seven shots a week and one is profound.
ASND.sound_bite: Seven shots a week becomes one. For kids who need it most.

DNTH.name: Dianthus Therapeutics
DNTH.big_picture: Next-generation complement antibody therapies for autoimmune neurological diseases like myasthenia gravis and CIDP.
DNTH.story: Dianthus Therapeutics is leading the next generation of complement antibody therapeutics. Their lead drug, claseprubart (DNTH103), is a highly selective antibody that binds only to the active form of C1s in the classical complement pathway. It's being tested in generalized myasthenia gravis (where a Phase 2 study showed statistically significant improvements with a safety profile comparable to placebo), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (the Phase 3 CAPTIVATE trial recently got an early GO decision based on a responder analysis), and multifocal motor neuropathy. A second candidate, DNTH212, is a bifunctional fusion protein being tested in lupus.
DNTH.sound_bite: Pick one lock — the active form of C1s — and don't touch the rest of the immune system.

PTGX.name: Protagonist Therapeutics
PTGX.big_picture: First-in-class peptide therapies — NDA filed for polycythemia vera with Takeda, JAK-free psoriasis pill approved with J&J.
PTGX.story: Protagonist Therapeutics develops first-in-class peptide drugs for immune and hematological diseases. Their lead candidate, rusfertide, is a hepcidin mimetic that targets red blood cell overproduction in polycythemia vera — a type of blood cancer. The Phase 3 VERIFY study met its primary and all four key secondary endpoints, with 61.9% of patients maintaining absence of phlebotomy eligibility through week 52 and a greater than 13-fold reduction in annual phlebotomy rate. Partner Takeda submitted the NDA to the FDA in January 2026. Separately, Protagonist's oral IL-23 receptor peptide, icotrokinra, was already approved by the FDA as ICOTYDE for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis, licensed to Johnson & Johnson.
PTGX.sound_bite: One approved drug with J&J, one NDA filed with Takeda. A rare small-cap biotech with real commercial traction.

NKTR.name: Nektar Therapeutics
NKTR.big_picture: First-in-class regulatory T-cell stimulator — hit primary endpoints in a 393-patient Phase 2b for severe atopic dermatitis.
NKTR.story: Nektar Therapeutics is developing rezpegaldesleukin (REZPEG), a first-in-class regulatory T-cell stimulator that works by targeting the IL-2 receptor complex and preferentially expanding Treg cells to restore immune balance. The global Phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study in 393 patients with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis met its primary and key secondary endpoints, with durable responses in the 36-week blinded maintenance period showing both monthly and quarterly dosing work. The FDA granted Fast Track designation for atopic dermatitis and separately for severe-to-very severe alopecia areata, and a Phase 2 trial in Type 1 diabetes is also underway.
NKTR.sound_bite: Restoring immune balance from the top of the pathway. And it hit its endpoints.

BOOT.name: Boot Barn
BOOT.big_picture: Outfits the American workforce with western and work boots, clothing, and gear.
BOOT.story: Boot Barn outfits the American workforce with western and work-related boots, clothing, and gear, serving ranchers, construction workers, and oil-field crews through over 400 stores with a fiercely loyal following.
BOOT.sound_bite: Every data center America builds gets built by people wearing boots from Boot Barn.

CELH.name: Celsius Holdings
CELH.big_picture: The healthier energy drink that became a cultural phenomenon with younger consumers.
CELH.story: Celsius Holdings has turned the energy-drink market upside down. While traditional brands competed on extreme caffeine and aggressive marketing, Celsius positioned itself as the healthier choice — no sugar, no artificial preservatives, clinically studied ingredients.
