The bet worked spectacularly: Celsius has gone from a niche brand to one of the top energy drinks in America, becoming a genuine cultural phenomenon among younger, health-conscious consumers.
CELH.sound_bite: Red Bull for people who read labels.

SAIA.name: Saia Inc.
SAIA.big_picture: One of the country's largest freight carriers — the quiet arteries of American commerce.
SAIA.story: Saia is one of the largest freight carriers in the country, moving shipments for thousands of businesses that need to get products from factories to stores to doorsteps. They've been steadily opening new terminals to improve speed and coverage — the quiet arteries of American commerce that most people never think about until a package doesn't arrive.
SAIA.sound_bite: The arteries of American commerce. Invisible until something stops.

UTI.name: Universal Technical Institute
UTI.big_picture: Trains the HVAC techs, diesel mechanics, welders, and electricians the country cannot build data centers without.
UTI.story: UTI addresses one of the most critical and overlooked gaps in the American economy: training skilled tradespeople. HVAC technicians, diesel mechanics, welders, electricians — you cannot build data centers, power plants, or hospitals without them. As the country embarks on the largest infrastructure buildout in a generation, UTI is one of the only companies positioned to help close the workforce gap.
UTI.sound_bite: You can't build data centers without welders. UTI trains the welders.

LINC.name: Lincoln Educational Services
LINC.big_picture: Lincoln Tech — career-oriented post-secondary education in skilled trades, automotive, and health sciences.
LINC.story: Lincoln Educational Services operates Lincoln Tech, a group of career-oriented post-secondary vocational schools with campuses across a dozen states. They train recent high school graduates and working adults in skilled trades, automotive, health sciences, and IT — the exact same thesis as UTI, targeting the same skills gap. Lincoln Tech was founded in 1946 to retrain WWII veterans and continues that mission today.
LINC.sound_bite: America has a trades gap. Lincoln Tech has been closing it since 1946.

LTH.name: Life Time Holdings
LTH.big_picture: Resort-style wellness campuses — pools, tennis, coworking, childcare — for people building their life around fitness.
LTH.story: Life Time Holdings operates large, resort-style wellness campuses with pools, tennis courts, coworking spaces, and children's programs under one roof, designed for people who want to build their entire daily routine around health and community.
LTH.sound_bite: Not a gym. A campus where your whole day can happen.

CAVA.name: CAVA Group
CAVA.big_picture: Largest Mediterranean fast-casual chain in the U.S. — scaling toward 1,000 restaurants by 2032.
CAVA.story: CAVA is the largest restaurant operator in the Mediterranean fast-casual category in the U.S., with around 400 company-owned restaurants and a target of 1,000 by 2032. The assembly-line format allows for over 17 billion meal combinations, catering to vegan, vegetarian, and keto diets. Q1 2025 revenue grew 28% with 24.4% restaurant-level margins — well ahead of larger competitors. A consumer packaged goods segment (dips and spreads) adds a second revenue stream and builds brand awareness in grocery.
CAVA.sound_bite: Mediterranean food, Chipotle model, 24% margins. The growth story is in the real estate pipeline.

COCO.name: The Vita Coco Company
COCO.big_picture: The dominant coconut water brand — 42% U.S. market share, growing internationally.
COCO.story: Vita Coco is the dominant coconut water brand in the U.S. with a 42% market share, now growing faster than almost any other beverage category. 2025 net sales grew 18% with the Vita Coco brand itself up 26% globally. Growth is driven by expanding household penetration (positioning as a sports-drink alternative), international expansion in Europe, and product innovation like Vita Coco Treats.
COCO.sound_bite: Coconut water became a real beverage category. Vita Coco owns it.

YOU.name: Clear Secure
YOU.big_picture: Biometric identity verification at airports — 30M+ members, expanding into a 'verify once, use everywhere' platform.
YOU.story: Clear Secure provides biometric identity verification through its signature blue lanes and biometric pods at airport checkpoints, letting 30+ million subscribers bypass regular security lines. The core business is a $199/year CLEAR Plus subscription, with revenue shared with airport partners. Beyond airports, Clear is expanding its CLEAR1 B2B platform into healthcare, financial services, and hospitality using a 'verify once, use everywhere' model.
YOU.sound_bite: Your face, your boarding pass. And soon your logins, your hotel check-in, and your doctor's visit.

SPHR.name: Sphere Entertainment
SPHR.big_picture: Operator of the Las Vegas Sphere — 17,600-seat immersive venue with a 16K wraparound screen, the top-grossing venue in the world.
SPHR.story: Sphere Entertainment operates the Las Vegas Sphere, a 17,600-seat immersive entertainment venue with a 16K resolution wraparound interior LED screen, speaker arrays using beamforming and wave-field synthesis, and 4D physical effects. The exterior is 580,000 sq ft of LED displays — the largest in the world — and pulls in separate advertising revenue. The venue was ranked #1 on Billboard's and Pollstar's 2025 lists of top-grossing venues worldwide, and the 'Wizard of Oz' immersive experience plus concert residencies are expanding utilization. A second U.S. Sphere is planned for National Harbor outside Washington, D.C., and a venue in Abu Dhabi is also in development. The company also owns MSG Networks, a legacy regional sports network in structural decline — one of the main investor debates around the stock.
SPHR.sound_bite: The most talked-about venue in the world. And there are more coming.

FROG.name: JFrog
FROG.big_picture: Manages the supply chain of software — makes sure no malicious code slips in between dev and production.
FROG.story: JFrog manages what you might call the supply chain of software. Just as a physical product moves from factory to warehouse to store shelf, software moves from a developer's computer through a series of testing and security stages before reaching the servers that run it in production. JFrog makes sure that pipeline runs smoothly and that no malicious code slips in along the way.
FROG.sound_bite: The factory floor for software. Inspection included.

DOCN.name: DigitalOcean
DOCN.big_picture: Developer-friendly cloud for startups and SMBs — AI revenue doubling for five straight quarters.
DOCN.story: DigitalOcean democratizes cloud and AI technology for developers, startups, and small businesses that can't afford the complexity of AWS, Azure, or GCP. The business is largely self-service — sign up on the website, deploy in minutes — which is exactly what smaller developers want. The AI pivot is working: AI-native revenue has doubled year-over-year for five straight quarters, customers with $100K+ ARR jumped 41% in a recent quarter, and customers with $1M+ ARR jumped 72%. The company is targeting a $251 billion addressable market of small-business infrastructure spend by 2028.
DOCN.sound_bite: AWS for everyone who doesn't want to read an AWS manual.

FSLY.name: Fastly
FSLY.big_picture: Programmable edge cloud platform — CDN + security + compute, concentrated in the most network-dense hubs.
FSLY.story: Fastly runs a programmable edge cloud platform combining content delivery, security, and compute. Unlike traditional CDNs that race to put servers in as many locations as possible, Fastly concentrates its servers in the most network-dense data centers and lets customers program on its platform, which delivers better performance on dynamic content and edge compute workloads. The growth strategy centers on three pillars: platform innovation, security expansion (higher-margin services that diversify revenue), and international growth in Asia-Pacific and Europe.
FSLY.sound_bite: Fewer, faster, smarter edge locations. Programmable end to end.

GWRE.name: Guidewire Software
GWRE.big_picture: The core operating system of the property & casualty insurance industry — 570+ insurers in 42 countries.
GWRE.story: Guidewire Software is the leading provider of cloud-based core systems for property and casualty (P&C) insurance. Their InsuranceSuite — ClaimCenter, PolicyCenter, and BillingCenter — is used by more than 570 insurers in 42 countries, from new ventures to giants like Allianz, AXA, and Liberty Mutual. The big story is the migration from on-premise to the Guidewire Cloud Platform, which lets insurers reduce infrastructure costs, improve disaster recovery, and integrate with AI and other digital tools. That multi-year transition is now hitting full stride, which is why Guidewire's growth has been reaccelerating.
GWRE.sound_bite: If you've ever filed an insurance claim, Guidewire was probably in the background.

TTAN.name: ServiceTitan
TTAN.big_picture: Cloud business management platform for skilled trades — plumbers, electricians, HVAC, landscapers.
TTAN.story: ServiceTitan provides a cloud-based business management platform for the skilled trades: plumbers, electricians, HVAC contractors, garage door installers, pest control, landscaping, and more. The software handles CRM, sales, customer service, marketing automation, e-commerce, and analytics — everything a tradesperson needs to run a business at scale, giving a family-owned contractor the same operational sophistication as a national chain. ServiceTitan booked $685 million in revenue for the 12 months ending July 2024 and went public on Nasdaq in December 2024 at $71 per share, well above the upwardly revised range.
TTAN.sound_bite: What Guidewire did for insurance, ServiceTitan is doing for the trades.

NAVN.name: Navan
NAVN.big_picture: All-in-one business travel, payments, and expense management — 10,000+ customers, $7.6B annual gross bookings.
NAVN.story: Navan is an all-in-one business travel, payments, and expense management platform that competes with both niche players like Expensify and enterprise heavyweights like Oracle and SAP. The pitch is traveler-first: make booking flights and hotels feel like a consumer app, then automate expense reconciliation on the back end with 24/7 support. Trailing 12-month revenue of $613M is growing 32%, across 10,000+ customers, with $7.6B in annual gross bookings (up 34%). Navan IPO'd on Nasdaq in October 2025 at $25/share and a $6.2B valuation, though shares dropped 20% on the first day of trading.
NAVN.sound_bite: Consumer-app business travel. Enterprise-scale expense automation.

HUT.name: Hut 8
HUT.big_picture: Bitcoin miner flexibly renting facilities to AI companies — same building, different customers.
HUT.story: Hut 8 operates large-scale facilities equipped for Bitcoin mining and has increasingly begun renting that same computing capacity to AI companies. The infrastructure is similar — power connections, cooling systems, dense racks of processors — so when Bitcoin prices are strong, they mine, and when AI companies need capacity, they rent it out. It's a flexible model.
HUT.sound_bite: When Bitcoin is hot, they mine. When AI is hot, they host. Same building.

WULF.name: TeraWulf
WULF.big_picture: Bitcoin miner running primarily on nuclear and hydro power — one of the greenest mining operations in the industry.
WULF.story: TeraWulf takes a similar approach to Hut 8 but with a distinctive emphasis on clean energy — their flagship facility runs primarily on nuclear and hydroelectric power, making them one of the greenest mining operations in the industry. Like Hut 8, they are expanding their AI hosting capabilities, recognizing that the same infrastructure built for cryptocurrency can serve the exploding demand for artificial intelligence computing.
WULF.sound_bite: The green Bitcoin miner. And an AI host on the side.

CIFR.name: Cipher Mining
CIFR.big_picture: Bitcoin miner pivoting hardest to AI — $5.5B AWS lease, $3B+ Fluidstack deals, 1GW West Texas HPC campus.
CIFR.story: Cipher Mining has leaned hardest into the AI pivot among Bitcoin miners. In late 2025 it signed a roughly $5.5 billion, 15-year lease with Amazon Web Services to deliver 300 MW of turnkey space and power for AI workloads starting in 2026. Separately, Cipher has a 168 MW, 10-year HPC agreement with Fluidstack worth approximately $3 billion in contracted revenue, plus a 56 MW Fluidstack expansion. They recently acquired the 200 MW 'Ulysses' site in Ohio with an AEP Ohio utility agreement, and took 95% majority control of Colchis, a 1-gigawatt HPC development in West Texas. Cipher is transforming into a hybrid bitcoin miner and 'power landlord' for AI data center tenants — and is increasingly being priced that way.
CIFR.sound_bite: A Bitcoin miner that just signed a $5.5B AWS lease. That's the whole story.

NVDA.name: NVIDIA
NVDA.big_picture: The dominant designer of GPUs that train and run almost every AI model in the world.
NVDA.story: NVIDIA designs the graphics processing units (GPUs) that have become the universal hardware for training and running artificial intelligence. What started as a chip for video games turned out to be the perfect parallel-computing engine for the math behind neural networks. Every major AI company on Earth — OpenAI, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Anthropic, xAI — runs its training on NVIDIA hardware, and the company has built a deep software moat around CUDA that competitors have spent years trying and failing to replicate. NVIDIA is the most valuable semiconductor company in the world, and arguably the most important.
NVDA.sound_bite: If AI is a gold rush, NVIDIA sells the only shovels that work.

AVGO.name: Broadcom
AVGO.big_picture: Designs custom AI chips for hyperscalers and dominates AI networking — plus a recurring software business in VMware.
AVGO.story: Broadcom is one of the most important companies in tech that most people have never heard of. It designs custom chips (XPUs) that companies like Google, Meta, and OpenAI use to train and run their AI models — think of it as the architect that designs each customer's AI engine, tailored to exactly what they need. It also builds the high-speed networking switches that let thousands of these chips talk to each other inside massive data centers. On top of all that, Broadcom owns VMware, a major software platform for running data centers more efficiently, creating a steady stream of subscription revenue alongside the chip business.
AVGO.sound_bite: The arms dealer of the AI revolution — it doesn't pick which AI model wins, it builds the custom hardware they all need.

AMD.name: Advanced Micro Devices
AMD.big_picture: The CPU and GPU challenger that finally caught Intel in servers and is now fighting NVIDIA in AI accelerators.
AMD.story: AMD spent years as a distant second in CPUs and GPUs, then under CEO Lisa Su became the credible alternative. Their EPYC server chips have been taking share from Intel in data centers for years, and their MI300 and MI350 line of AI accelerators have become the only Western alternative to NVIDIA for AI training and inference. The opportunity is enormous because hyperscalers want a second source for AI silicon, and AMD is the only one that exists at scale.
AMD.sound_bite: The only credible second source to NVIDIA — and the cloud wants two suppliers.

MU.name: Micron Technology
MU.big_picture: One of three remaining DRAM memory makers, riding the surge in HBM memory needed by AI accelerators.
MU.story: Micron is one of only three companies left in the world that make DRAM memory — the working memory inside every computer, server, and phone. The AI boom has driven explosive demand for High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), the specialty stacked DRAM that sits next to NVIDIA and AMD's AI accelerators and feeds them data. HBM is the bottleneck for AI chip performance, which gives Micron unusual pricing power in a historically cyclical industry.
MU.sound_bite: AI chips are useless without memory. Micron makes the memory.

SNDK.name: SanDisk
SNDK.big_picture: Pure-play NAND flash memory leader, spun out of Western Digital in 2025.
SNDK.story: SanDisk completed its separation from Western Digital in February 2025 and became an independent public company focused entirely on NAND flash memory and SSDs. The pure-play structure unlocks pricing discipline and capital allocation that the combined company never had. NAND demand has surged with the AI buildout — every AI inference workload pulls model weights from flash storage, and storage has become a more strategic part of the AI stack than anyone expected. SanDisk shares are up over 5x since the spinoff.
SNDK.sound_bite: The flash memory pure-play that woke up at exactly the right moment.

LRCX.name: Lam Research
LRCX.big_picture: Critical semiconductor equipment for etch and deposition — the steps that physically build chip layers.
LRCX.story: Lam Research builds the machines that etch microscopic patterns into silicon wafers and deposit the thin films that form chip layers. Every advanced semiconductor fab on Earth uses Lam equipment, and the transition to 3D chip architectures (NAND stacking, gate-all-around transistors) is making etch and deposition even more critical to chip performance.
LRCX.sound_bite: Every chip layer that gets etched or deposited goes through a Lam machine.

KLAC.name: KLA Corporation
KLAC.big_picture: Process control and inspection — the equipment that catches defects during chip manufacturing.
KLAC.story: KLA makes the inspection and metrology systems that semiconductor manufacturers use to find defects in chips during production. As feature sizes shrink to single-digit nanometers, defect detection has become one of the most valuable parts of the entire fab process — a single missed defect can ruin a million-dollar wafer. KLA is the dominant player and operates at gross margins higher than almost any other equipment vendor.
KLAC.sound_bite: The microscope that saves a billion-dollar fab from one bad layer.

ADI.name: Analog Devices
ADI.big_picture: Analog and mixed-signal chips that bridge the physical world to digital systems.
ADI.story: Analog Devices makes the chips that convert real-world signals — temperature, pressure, motion, sound, light — into the digital data that computers can process, and back again. Every electric vehicle, factory robot, medical device, and industrial control system depends on analog chips. The business is sticky because analog designs are highly differentiated and hard to switch.
ADI.sound_bite: The digital revolution still has to talk to the physical world. ADI does the translating.

MPWR.name: Monolithic Power Systems
MPWR.big_picture: Power management chips that control electricity inside everything from AI servers to EVs.
MPWR.story: Monolithic Power Systems designs the chips that manage power delivery inside electronic systems — converting voltages, regulating current, and protecting against surges. As AI servers, electric vehicles, and 5G networks all demand more power and more efficiency, Monolithic's high-density power management ICs have become essential. The company has been one of the fastest-growing semiconductor names of the last decade.
MPWR.sound_bite: Every watt of electricity inside an AI server passes through chips like Monolithic's.

AEHR.name: Aehr Test Systems
AEHR.big_picture: Burn-in test systems for power semiconductors — critical for silicon carbide chips in EVs.
AEHR.story: Aehr Test Systems builds the wafer-level burn-in systems that semiconductor companies use to stress-test chips before shipping them. Their FOX systems became essential as silicon carbide (SiC) power chips for electric vehicles took off — SiC chips are expensive and field failures are unacceptable, so manufacturers test every wafer. Aehr is small but dominant in its niche.
AEHR.sound_bite: Every silicon carbide chip in an EV gets stress-tested on an Aehr machine.

TER.name: Teradyne
TER.big_picture: Automatic test equipment for semiconductors and industrial automation robots.
TER.story: Teradyne sells the automatic test equipment (ATE) that semiconductor manufacturers use to verify every chip before it ships, and also owns Universal Robots, one of the leading collaborative-robot brands. The semiconductor business benefits from rising chip complexity (more tests per chip), and the robotics business benefits from the labor crunch in manufacturing.
TER.sound_bite: Test the chips, then test the robots that pick up the chips.

AMBA.name: Ambarella
AMBA.big_picture: Computer-vision and AI inference chips for cameras, drones, and self-driving systems.
AMBA.story: Ambarella designs low-power AI vision processors — chips that can run computer-vision and machine-learning models inside cameras, drones, security systems, and increasingly automotive ADAS and robotics. Their CV-series chips are designed specifically to do AI inference at the edge with tiny power budgets, which is exactly what self-driving and embedded AI applications need.
AMBA.sound_bite: AI inference inside the camera, not the cloud.

ANET.name: Arista Networks
ANET.big_picture: AI networking switches — the high-speed highways inside hyperscale AI data centers.
ANET.story: Arista Networks makes the high-speed networking switches and software that connect everything inside the world's largest data centers. If AI chips are the workers, Arista builds the roads between them — and those roads need to be extraordinarily fast when thousands of chips are training an AI model together. Arista's switches and its operating system (EOS) are considered the gold standard by Microsoft, Meta, and Google, which is why the company has overtaken Cisco as the leading data center networking provider.
ANET.sound_bite: You can't have an AI revolution without building the network to carry it. Arista is wiring it all together.

GLW.name: Corning
GLW.big_picture: Optical fiber, ceramic substrates for chip packaging, and the Gorilla Glass on every modern phone.
GLW.story: Corning makes the optical fiber that carries 90% of the world's internet traffic, the ceramic substrates that go inside advanced chip packaging, the Gorilla Glass that protects smartphone screens, and the precision glass used in semiconductor lithography. The company has been quietly enabling the modern internet for decades, and the AI buildout is driving a massive new wave of optical fiber demand for data center interconnects.
GLW.sound_bite: When the world needs more glass, Corning has been making it for 175 years.

APH.name: Amphenol
APH.big_picture: One of the world's largest connector manufacturers — the physical interfaces that link electronics together.
APH.story: Amphenol makes the connectors and interconnect systems that link electronic devices to each other and to the outside world. Every server, cell tower, electric vehicle, jet, and missile uses thousands of Amphenol's connectors. The business has been a quiet compounder for decades because connectors are sticky (qualifying a new vendor is expensive) and constantly need upgrades as data rates and power requirements rise.
APH.sound_bite: If two electronic things plug into each other, there's a good chance Amphenol made the plug.

TEL.name: TE Connectivity
TEL.big_picture: Connectors and sensors for cars, factories, energy systems, and medical devices.
TEL.story: TE Connectivity is Amphenol's biggest competitor and runs a similar business: connectors, sensors, and electrical components that go into transportation, industrial, and communications systems. The company has particularly strong positions in automotive (where every EV needs many more connectors than an internal combustion car) and in industrial automation.
TEL.sound_bite: The other connector giant. Two of them quietly own most of the physical interconnect.

LITE.name: Lumentum
LITE.big_picture: Optical components for high-speed networking and 3D sensing — including the lasers in iPhones.
LITE.story: Lumentum designs the optical and photonic components that go inside data center networking equipment, and the 3D sensing lasers used in smartphones and increasingly in automotive lidar. The data center optical business benefits directly from AI buildouts, and the company has positioned itself as a key supplier to NVIDIA and the hyperscalers.
LITE.sound_bite: When you need to convert electrons into photons inside a data center, Lumentum is one of the few choices.

AAOI.name: Applied Optoelectronics
AAOI.big_picture: Fiber-optic transceivers and components for data centers and broadband networks.
AAOI.story: Applied Optoelectronics designs and manufactures the fiber-optic modules that data centers use to connect servers and switches at very high speeds. The company has been winning new design wins for next-generation 800G transceivers as hyperscalers upgrade their networks to handle AI traffic.
AAOI.sound_bite: When data needs to ride a beam of light across a data center, AAOI makes the converter.

OUST.name: Ouster
OUST.big_picture: Digital lidar sensors for autonomous vehicles, smart infrastructure, and robotics.
OUST.story: Ouster makes solid-state digital lidar sensors that let machines see the world in 3D. Their sensors are used in self-driving vehicles, smart cities, robotics, defense, and security applications. Lidar has been a notoriously difficult business — many companies failed — but Ouster has built a real product portfolio and is one of the few public pure-play lidar names with meaningful revenue.
OUST.sound_bite: If a machine needs to see in 3D, Ouster sells the eyes.

CALX.name: Calix
CALX.big_picture: Networking equipment that brings high-speed broadband to small and rural internet providers.
CALX.story: Calix builds the networking equipment that lets small, local internet providers deliver fast, reliable broadband to rural and underserved communities. Their cloud-based software gives a small-town provider the sophistication of a tech giant, helping close the digital divide one community at a time.
CALX.sound_bite: The Cisco for small-town America's broadband.

HWM.name: Howmet Aerospace
HWM.big_picture: Specialty metal parts for jet engines and gas turbines — sole-source on many of the most critical components.
HWM.story: Howmet makes the incredibly specialized metal parts that go inside jet engines, airframe structures, and industrial gas turbines — components that must withstand extreme temperatures, pressures, and stresses. Engine blades, structural fasteners, and forged rings from Howmet are found in virtually every commercial and military aircraft flying today, and increasingly in the gas turbines powering AI data centers. Many of the parts are sole-source, meaning the customer has no approved alternative — which gives Howmet exceptional pricing power and customer stickiness.
HWM.sound_bite: Every time a jet engine fires up or a gas turbine spins, there's a Howmet part inside doing the impossible — and no one else knows how to make it.

CW.name: Curtiss-Wright
CW.big_picture: Highly engineered components for nuclear submarines, defense systems, and commercial nuclear plants.
CW.story: Curtiss-Wright traces its lineage to the Wright Brothers and now makes precision components for the most demanding environments imaginable — nuclear submarine reactors, naval propulsion systems, military aircraft, and commercial nuclear power plants. As nuclear power gains favor for grid decarbonization and AI data center supply, Curtiss-Wright's nuclear franchise is in unusual demand.
CW.sound_bite: The Wright Brothers' company, now keeping nuclear submarines and reactors running.

IEX.name: IDEX Corporation
IEX.big_picture: Precision pumps, valves, and fluid-handling equipment for industrial, life-science, and fire-rescue markets.
IEX.story: IDEX is a diversified specialty industrial company that makes precision pumps, valves, and fluid handling equipment for highly engineered niches. Their products go into pharmaceutical manufacturing, semiconductor fabs, fire trucks, and industrial automation. The business is a collection of small market-leading franchises, each with high margins because the products are mission-critical and hard to substitute.
IEX.sound_bite: Boring industrial niches, run with discipline. The compounder you've never heard of.

VRT.name: Vertiv Holdings
VRT.big_picture: Power and cooling infrastructure for data centers — the picks and shovels of AI buildouts.
VRT.story: Vertiv makes the power distribution units, uninterruptible power supplies, racks, and cooling systems that data centers depend on. As AI workloads have driven a new wave of data center construction, Vertiv has become one of the most direct beneficiaries — every new MW of AI capacity needs Vertiv equipment to run reliably. The company sells liquid cooling systems specifically designed for high-density AI racks.
VRT.sound_bite: The infrastructure inside the data center, not the chips. Just as essential.

SXI.name: Standex International
SXI.big_picture: Diversified specialty industrial — electronics, engraving, scientific products, and engineering technologies.
SXI.story: Standex is a small diversified industrial holding a portfolio of engineered products businesses, including specialty electronics components, custom engraving for consumer brands, and scientific cold storage equipment. Each segment is small but holds a strong niche position. The company has been a quiet compounder run with capital discipline.
SXI.sound_bite: A handful of small industrial niches, each with leadership in its lane.

TRNS.name: Transcat
TRNS.big_picture: Calibration services for the precision instruments that quality-critical industries depend on.
TRNS.story: Transcat provides accredited calibration services for the precision measuring equipment used in life sciences, aerospace, semiconductors, and other regulated industries. When the FDA, FAA, or a chip-fab QA team requires that an instrument be traceable to NIST standards, Transcat is one of the largest providers in North America. The business is recurring (calibration cycles repeat) and growing as regulation gets more rigorous.
TRNS.sound_bite: The company that makes sure the instruments measuring everything are themselves accurate.

XMTR.name: Xometry
XMTR.big_picture: AI-powered marketplace that connects engineers to thousands of small custom-manufacturing shops.
XMTR.story: Xometry has built something genuinely new: an AI-powered online marketplace for custom manufacturing. An engineer uploads a 3D design file, and Xometry's system instantly analyzes the geometry, picks the right manufacturing method (machining, 3D printing, injection molding, sheet metal), prices it, and routes the order to the best shop in a network of thousands. Finished parts arrive in days. It's bringing the convenience of online shopping to an industry that historically required weeks of phone calls.
XMTR.sound_bite: Amazon for custom-manufactured parts. With AI doing the routing.

GEV.name: GE Vernova
GEV.big_picture: The most important power infrastructure company in the world — gas turbines, wind, and grid equipment.
GEV.story: GE Vernova is the energy company spun out of General Electric in 2024. It builds and services the gas turbines that generate roughly 25% of the world's electricity, manufactures wind turbines, and provides the grid equipment (transformers, switchgear, circuit breakers) that moves power from where it's made to where it's used. With AI data centers driving urgent demand for new power generation, GE Vernova's order backlog has exploded past $150 billion. Management expects at least $22 billion in cumulative free cash flow by 2028.
GEV.sound_bite: AI needs electricity before it needs anything else — GE Vernova is the company that keeps the lights on and the data centers humming.

LEU.name: Centrus Energy
LEU.big_picture: The only U.S. producer of high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) — the fuel for next-gen reactors.
LEU.story: Centrus operates the only U.S. facility licensed by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission to produce high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) — the fuel that next-generation small modular reactors and microreactors will need to run. As of 2026, only Russia and China have HALEU production at scale. With the U.S. ban on Russian uranium imports and the resurgence of nuclear power for AI, Centrus is the only domestic supplier with a working facility, expanding under DOE contract.
LEU.sound_bite: Next-gen nuclear runs on HALEU. Centrus is the only American company making it.

LNG.name: Cheniere Energy
LNG.big_picture: The largest U.S. exporter of liquefied natural gas, supplying global gas turbine power generation.
LNG.story: Cheniere Energy is the largest U.S. exporter of liquefied natural gas. The company runs massive liquefaction terminals on the Gulf Coast that turn cheap U.S. natural gas into a liquid that can be loaded onto ships and sold globally. As Europe replaced Russian gas and Asia electrified, Cheniere's long-term LNG supply contracts became some of the most valuable energy assets in the world. The thesis dovetails with the AI power story — LNG fuels the gas turbines that fuel the data centers.
LNG.sound_bite: America's biggest LNG exporter. The pipeline from cheap U.S. gas to global power generation.

FTI.name: TechnipFMC
FTI.big_picture: Subsea oil and gas equipment — the integrator of offshore energy production systems.
FTI.story: TechnipFMC designs and integrates the subsea production systems that get oil and gas out of the seafloor. Their integrated subsea production systems (iEPCI) bundle the equipment, engineering, procurement, construction, and installation into a single contract — a model competitors have struggled to copy. Offshore drilling has come back into favor as energy security concerns rose, and TechnipFMC has been one of the biggest beneficiaries.
FTI.sound_bite: Drilling oil and gas a mile under the ocean is hard. TechnipFMC integrates the whole system.

EME.name: EMCOR Group
EME.big_picture: One of the largest mechanical and electrical construction contractors in the U.S.
EME.story: EMCOR is one of the largest U.S. providers of mechanical and electrical construction, energy infrastructure, and facilities services. They install and maintain the HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and fire-protection systems inside large commercial, industrial, healthcare, and data-center facilities. As data center construction has surged, EMCOR has become one of the most important contractors in America.
EME.sound_bite: When a hyperscaler builds a new data center, EMCOR does much of the work inside it.

PWR.name: Quanta Services
PWR.big_picture: The largest U.S. specialty contractor for electric power, pipeline, and communications infrastructure.
PWR.story: Quanta Services builds and maintains the electric grid, oil and gas pipelines, telecommunications networks, and renewable energy infrastructure across North America. They are the contractor utilities call when a new transmission line, substation, or large solar farm needs to be built. With the U.S. grid in the middle of its largest expansion in decades — driven by AI power demand, EV charging, and renewables — Quanta has multi-year backlog visibility.
PWR.sound_bite: The biggest grid contractor in America — building the wires that carry the AI power.

APG.name: APi Group
APG.big_picture: Life safety, security, and specialty industrial services to commercial and industrial buildings.
APG.story: APi Group provides safety services (fire protection, alarm systems, security) and specialty industrial services across more than a dozen countries. The business is recurring — every commercial building needs fire alarms inspected and sprinklers serviced — and APi has consolidated a fragmented industry through dozens of acquisitions.
APG.sound_bite: Fire alarms, sprinklers, security systems. Recurring revenue at industrial scale.

MYRG.name: MYR Group
MYRG.big_picture: Specialty electrical contractor focused on transmission, distribution, and commercial-industrial electrical work.
MYRG.story: MYR Group builds high-voltage transmission lines, substations, and distribution systems for utilities, plus large commercial and industrial electrical projects. The company sits in exactly the right place for the U.S. grid expansion driven by AI power, electrification, and renewables.
MYRG.sound_bite: When a utility needs a new transmission line built, MYR is one of the few who can do it.

PRIM.name: Primoris Services
PRIM.big_picture: Specialty infrastructure construction across utilities, energy, and renewables.
PRIM.story: Primoris is a specialty contractor for the utility, energy, and renewables sectors — building solar farms, electrical transmission, gas distribution, and pipeline systems. The business has shifted significantly toward renewables and electrical work as those markets grew faster than legacy oil and gas pipeline construction.
PRIM.sound_bite: Solar farms, transmission lines, gas distribution — the contractor for what the energy transition actually needs built.

WLDN.name: Willdan Group
WLDN.big_picture: Engineering and consulting for utilities and municipalities to modernize the grid and improve energy efficiency.
WLDN.story: Willdan helps utilities, cities, and other public agencies design plans for grid modernization, energy efficiency programs, and electrification. They figure out where new capacity is needed, how existing buildings can use energy more efficiently, and how the transition to greater electrification can happen in an orderly way.
WLDN.sound_bite: The brain that helps cities and utilities figure out how to upgrade the grid.

LHX.name: L3Harris Technologies
LHX.big_picture: Defense electronics and communications systems — radios, sensors, satellites, and electronic warfare.
LHX.story: L3Harris is one of the largest U.S. defense contractors, focused on tactical communications (radios used by U.S. and allied troops), space systems (satellites and payloads), and electronic warfare. The company has been winning meaningful share in space and missile-defense as the Pentagon shifts spending toward those domains.
LHX.sound_bite: When U.S. troops need to talk to each other under fire, they're using an L3Harris radio.

GE.name: GE Aerospace
GE.big_picture: The world's largest jet engine manufacturer, post-spin from General Electric.
GE.story: After spinning out GE Vernova in 2024, the rest of General Electric became GE Aerospace — the world's largest jet engine manufacturer. GE engines power most of the commercial aircraft fleet through partnerships with Boeing and Airbus, plus a huge military business. The aftermarket services franchise (engine overhauls, parts, maintenance) generates the bulk of the profit and is highly recurring.
GE.sound_bite: Most jet engines flying today were built by GE — and overhauling them generates the real money.

AXON.name: Axon Enterprise
AXON.big_picture: TASERs, body cameras, and the cloud software that runs modern police departments.
AXON.story: Axon makes the TASER non-lethal weapon, body cameras, and the Evidence.com cloud platform that police departments use to manage all their video, records, and reports. The business is a sticky software-and-hardware bundle — once a department standardizes on Axon, the switching costs are enormous. The company has been one of the best-performing names in the entire market over the last decade.
AXON.sound_bite: TASERs and body cameras are the trojan horse. The cloud subscription is the business.

GILT.name: Gilat Satellite Networks
GILT.big_picture: Satellite communications equipment for in-flight connectivity, defense, and rural broadband.
GILT.story: Gilat provides the satellite ground equipment, antennas, and modems that connect remote terminals to satellite networks. Their gear is used in commercial in-flight Wi-Fi, military satcom, maritime, and rural broadband. As low-earth-orbit satellite constellations like Starlink and Project Kuiper proliferate, Gilat is selling the ground infrastructure that talks to them.
GILT.sound_bite: If a satellite is talking to the ground, there's a good chance Gilat made the gear on the ground.

BKSY.name: BlackSky Technology
BKSY.big_picture: Real-time satellite imaging and intelligence for defense and intelligence customers.
BKSY.story: BlackSky operates a constellation of small earth-observation satellites that can revisit the same spot on Earth multiple times per day, then run AI analytics on the imagery to detect activity in real time. Their customers are primarily defense and intelligence agencies that need persistent monitoring of specific locations — bases, ships, borders, contested zones.
BKSY.sound_bite: Watch any spot on Earth, in real time, and let AI tell you what's changed.

AIR.name: AAR Corp
AIR.big_picture: Aerospace aftermarket — parts, repair, and supply chain services for commercial and military aircraft.
AIR.story: AAR provides aftermarket support for commercial airlines and government/defense customers — parts supply, MRO (maintenance, repair, overhaul), and integrated supply chain management. As aircraft fleets age and flight hours grow, the aftermarket has become structurally larger than new aircraft sales. AAR is one of the largest independent aftermarket players.
AIR.sound_bite: When an airline or air force needs parts and repairs to keep planes flying, AAR is one of the first calls.

TATT.name: TAT Technologies
TATT.big_picture: Aerospace heat exchangers and aftermarket repair for commercial and military aircraft.
TATT.story: TAT Technologies makes heat exchangers and other thermal management components for commercial and military aircraft, plus provides MRO services. They have particular strength in aftermarket repair of legacy components where newer competitors have not bothered to qualify.
TATT.sound_bite: Tiny aerospace niche, dominant position, recurring repair revenue.

ONDS.name: Ondas Holdings
ONDS.big_picture: Autonomous drones and counter-drone systems for defense, infrastructure, and border security.
ONDS.story: Ondas builds autonomous drone systems and counter-drone defenses through its subsidiaries American Robotics, Airobotics, and Apeiro Motion. Their Optimus System is the first FAA-certified small UAS for automated security and data capture, and their Iron Drone Raider is a counter-drone platform. In 2025, Ondas won a major government tender to deploy thousands of drones for border protection, plus contracts for counter-UAS at major European airports. Revenue grew 605% in 2025.
ONDS.sound_bite: Drones to deploy and drones to shoot down other drones.

OSIS.name: OSI Systems
OSIS.big_picture: Security inspection and screening systems — the X-ray and CT machines at airports, borders, and ports.
OSIS.story: OSI Systems builds the X-ray and CT-based screening systems used by airports, customs agencies, ports, and large public venues to detect threats and contraband. Their Rapiscan brand is one of the most familiar in the space. The business benefits from rising global defense and security spending plus the steady upgrade cycles of legacy airport equipment.
OSIS.sound_bite: Every bag at the airport that goes through an X-ray belt — there's a decent chance OSI built the belt.

LASR.name: nLight
LASR.big_picture: High-power lasers for advanced manufacturing, microfabrication, and directed-energy defense weapons.
LASR.story: nLight designs and builds the high-power semiconductor and fiber lasers used in industrial cutting, 3D printing, microfabrication, and increasingly in directed-energy weapons for defense. The defense business has become the most exciting growth driver as the Pentagon invests heavily in laser-based counter-drone and missile-defense systems.
LASR.sound_bite: The same laser tech that cuts metal in a factory is starting to shoot down drones.

IDXX.name: IDEXX Laboratories
IDXX.big_picture: Diagnostics for the veterinary market — point-of-care analyzers, reference labs, and software.
IDXX.story: IDEXX is the dominant diagnostics company for the veterinary industry. They make the point-of-care blood and urine analyzers that sit inside vet clinics, plus run reference laboratories, plus sell practice management software. The pet healthcare market has grown structurally as pet owners spend more on their animals, and IDEXX has compounded for decades on that secular trend.
IDXX.sound_bite: Pets get sick too. IDEXX runs the lab tests.

WGS.name: GeneDx Holdings
WGS.big_picture: Whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing for diagnosing rare childhood genetic diseases.
WGS.story: GeneDx sequences the complete human genome to diagnose rare genetic diseases, especially in children. For families who have spent years visiting specialist after specialist without answers, a single test can finally reveal the underlying cause and open the door to targeted treatment. The company has the largest pediatric exome database in the world, which gives its tests an interpretation advantage no one else can match.
WGS.sound_bite: The end of years of unanswered questions for families with sick children.

BLLN.name: BillionToOne
BLLN.big_picture: Molecular diagnostics for prenatal testing and oncology, using a quantitative platform to detect rare mutations.
BLLN.story: BillionToOne uses its proprietary QCT (Quantitative Counting Templates) platform to detect single-molecule changes in DNA — much more sensitive than older approaches. Their UNITY prenatal tests can screen for fetal genetic conditions and Rh blood type compatibility from a single blood draw, and their Northstar oncology tests are growing fast in tumor profiling. Revenue grew over 100% in 2025, with $430-445M guided for 2026.
BLLN.sound_bite: Counting individual DNA molecules to catch what other tests miss.

CBLL.name: CeriBell
CBLL.big_picture: Point-of-care EEG headband that lets non-specialists detect seizures in hospital ICUs.
CBLL.story: CeriBell makes a portable EEG (electroencephalogram) headband that any nurse can put on a patient at the bedside, eliminating the multi-hour wait for a neurologist and a traditional EEG cart. The device uses AI to detect seizures in real time. ICUs have embraced it because undetected seizures are common in critically ill patients and devastating when missed. CeriBell also has FDA Breakthrough Device designation for stroke detection.
CBLL.sound_bite: EEG that doesn't need a neurologist to set up — and AI that flags the seizure.

HTFL.name: HeartFlow
HTFL.big_picture: AI that builds a 3D model of a patient's heart arteries from a CT scan to find blockages without invasive testing.
HTFL.story: HeartFlow uses artificial intelligence to create a detailed 3D model of a patient's heart arteries from a standard CT scan. The model shows exactly where blood is flowing freely and where blockages are restricting it. Traditionally, answering that question required threading a thin tube through a blood vessel into the heart. HeartFlow can often provide the same answer from a simple, non-invasive scan.
HTFL.sound_bite: Find heart blockages without threading a tube through someone's artery.

TWST.name: Twist Bioscience
TWST.big_picture: Synthetic DNA at industrial scale — for biotech research, drug discovery, and DNA data storage.
TWST.story: Twist Bioscience makes synthetic DNA on silicon chips at unprecedented scale and quality. Researchers and biotech companies use Twist DNA for gene synthesis, antibody discovery, and increasingly to store digital data in DNA strands (which are millions of times denser than any electronic medium). The company is a key supplier to nearly every modern biotech research lab.
TWST.sound_bite: Industrial-scale custom DNA. The toolmaker for the biotech revolution.

TXG.name: 10x Genomics
TXG.big_picture: Single-cell sequencing platforms that let researchers read DNA, RNA, and proteins one cell at a time.
TXG.story: 10x Genomics builds the instruments and chemistry that let researchers analyze biology at the resolution of individual cells. Traditional sequencing reads the average across millions of cells — 10x lets researchers see exactly what each cell is doing, which is the only way to understand many diseases. Almost every major academic and pharmaceutical research lab has a 10x instrument.
TXG.sound_bite: Reading biology one cell at a time. The microscope of the genomics era.

ISRG.name: Intuitive Surgical
ISRG.big_picture: The dominant maker of robotic surgery systems — the da Vinci robot.
ISRG.story: Intuitive Surgical makes the da Vinci robotic surgery system, the dominant platform for minimally invasive surgery. Once a hospital buys a $2 million robot and trains its surgeons on it, the recurring revenue from instruments, accessories, and service makes the install base extraordinarily valuable. The newer da Vinci 5 system is driving an upgrade cycle, and the company is expanding into new surgical specialties.
ISRG.sound_bite: The razor-and-blade model, applied to the operating room.

AORT.name: Artivion
AORT.big_picture: Heart valves and aortic repair products for cardiovascular surgery.
AORT.story: Artivion (formerly CryoLife) makes the heart valves, aortic stent grafts, and surgical repair products that cardiac surgeons use in complex aortic procedures. The business is small but holds strong positions in particular niches like aortic dissections and pediatric cardiac surgery.
AORT.sound_bite: Specialty cardiac repair, where there's no margin for error.

LLY.name: Eli Lilly
LLY.big_picture: The most valuable healthcare company in the world — GLP-1 leader and one of the deepest pipelines in pharma.
LLY.story: Eli Lilly has become the most valuable pharmaceutical company in the world on the strength of Mounjaro and Zepbound, the GLP-1 drugs that treat diabetes and obesity. The market for these drugs is unprecedented — tens of millions of patients potentially served, with multi-year demand visibility. Behind the GLP-1 franchise, Lilly has one of the deepest pipelines in pharma, with programs in Alzheimer's (Kisunla), oncology, immunology, and rare disease.
LLY.sound_bite: GLP-1 made Lilly the most valuable healthcare company on Earth. The pipeline says it stays there.

VRTX.name: Vertex Pharmaceuticals
VRTX.big_picture: Effectively cured cystic fibrosis. Now expanding into pain, kidney disease, and gene therapy.
VRTX.story: Vertex built the cystic fibrosis franchise that effectively cured most CF patients with their Trikafta combination drug — a stunning scientific achievement. The next chapter is harder: non-opioid pain (suzetrigine, marketed as Journavx), kidney disease (inaxaplin), Type 1 diabetes (cell therapy), and Casgevy, the first approved CRISPR gene-editing therapy for sickle cell disease, developed in partnership with CRISPR Therapeutics.
VRTX.sound_bite: Cured cystic fibrosis. Now trying to do it for pain, sickle cell, and diabetes.

GILD.name: Gilead Sciences
GILD.big_picture: Built the franchise that turned HIV from a death sentence into a manageable condition.
GILD.story: Gilead is the company that turned HIV into a chronic, manageable condition with its franchise of antiretroviral therapies. The HIV business still generates the bulk of revenue and continues to grow with longer-acting injectables like Sunlenca. Gilead has been investing heavily in oncology (Trodelvy, cell therapies) and inflammation to diversify beyond the core franchise.
GILD.sound_bite: Turned HIV into a manageable condition. Now trying to do the same for cancer.

ARGX.name: argenx
ARGX.big_picture: Created Vyvgart — the first FcRn-blocker — and is expanding it across half a dozen autoimmune diseases.
ARGX.story: argenx created Vyvgart (efgartigimod), the first approved FcRn blocker, which has become the new standard of care for generalized myasthenia gravis. The mechanism — temporarily reducing pathogenic antibodies — applies to many other autoimmune diseases, and argenx is running trials in CIDP, ITP, pemphigus, and several more. The company has built an impressive commercial organization for a Belgium-based biotech and is one of the most respected names in autoimmune drug development.
ARGX.sound_bite: One drug, one mechanism, half a dozen autoimmune diseases. Vyvgart is rewriting the playbook.

INSM.name: Insmed
INSM.big_picture: Building a franchise in serious lung disease — Arikayce for NTM infections plus brensocatib for bronchiectasis.
INSM.story: Insmed makes Arikayce, an inhaled antibiotic for serious nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) lung infections that previously required months of intravenous antibiotics. The next-generation drug is brensocatib, a DPP-1 inhibitor for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis that hit its Phase 3 trial — potentially a multi-billion-dollar opportunity in a disease with no approved treatment today.
INSM.sound_bite: Lung diseases that nobody else is treating. And one big late-stage drug.

NAMS.name: NewAmsterdam Pharma
NAMS.big_picture: Obicetrapib — an oral CETP inhibitor that cut LDL cholesterol by 33% in Phase 3, partnered with Menarini in Europe.
NAMS.story: NewAmsterdam is bringing obicetrapib to market — a once-daily oral CETP inhibitor that reduced LDL cholesterol by 33% in the Phase 3 BROADWAY trial and showed an exploratory 21% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events. Marketing applications have been accepted by the European Medicines Agency, with a decision expected 2H26. Partner Menarini holds European commercial rights, and a U.S. cardiovascular outcomes trial (PREVAIL) is ongoing.
NAMS.sound_bite: If obicetrapib gets approved, it could be the biggest cardiovascular drug launch of the decade.

MLYS.name: Mineralys Therapeutics
MLYS.big_picture: Lorundrostat — a novel oral drug for treatment-resistant hypertension.
MLYS.story: Mineralys is developing lorundrostat, an aldosterone synthase inhibitor for patients whose high blood pressure does not respond to standard medications — one of the largest unmet needs in cardiovascular medicine. Phase 2 data has been encouraging, and Phase 3 trials are underway.
MLYS.sound_bite: The blood pressure pill that works on patients other pills can't reach.

PRAX.name: Praxis Precision Medicines
PRAX.big_picture: Late-stage drugs for essential tremor and epilepsy — neurological disorders with limited options.
PRAX.story: Praxis Precision Medicines develops therapies for central nervous system disorders, with late-stage programs in essential tremor (ulixacaltamide) and epilepsy. Both indications are large markets where existing drugs have meaningful limitations, and Praxis has shown encouraging Phase 2 and 3 data.
PRAX.sound_bite: The hands stop shaking. The seizures stop. That's the goal.

IMCR.name: Immunocore Holdings
IMCR.big_picture: Developed the first approved TCR therapy — Kimmtrak for a rare eye cancer — and is expanding the platform.
IMCR.story: Immunocore built the first approved T cell receptor (TCR) therapy, Kimmtrak (tebentafusp), for a rare metastatic eye cancer (uveal melanoma). The TCR platform engages a patient's T cells and redirects them against cancer in a way conventional immunotherapy cannot. Beyond Kimmtrak, Immunocore is advancing TCR therapies in skin cancer, lung cancer, and HIV.
IMCR.sound_bite: The first commercial TCR therapy. The platform behind it might do much more.

FBRX.name: Forte Biosciences
FBRX.big_picture: Targeting autoimmune diseases through a novel anti-CD122 antibody.
FBRX.story: Forte Biosciences develops antibody therapies for autoimmune diseases, with its lead program targeting CD122 — a receptor involved in the activation of pathogenic immune cells. The approach offers potential advantages over older biologics in selectivity and durability of effect.
FBRX.sound_bite: A new mechanism for autoimmune disease, in early clinical work.

AAPL.name: Apple
AAPL.big_picture: Puts a supercomputer in a billion pockets — and sells the services that run on it.
AAPL.story: Apple is the most valuable consumer technology company in the world. The iPhone is the highest-margin consumer product ever built, and the broader ecosystem (Mac, iPad, Watch, AirPods, Services) compounds around it. The Services business — App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, advertising, payments — has become a recurring-revenue franchise larger than most Fortune 500 companies on its own. Apple's vertical integration and chip design (M-series silicon) keep extending the moat.
AAPL.sound_bite: A billion phones, a software ecosystem nobody else can replicate, and the highest-margin consumer business in history.

AMZN.name: Amazon
AMZN.big_picture: The everything store, the cloud that hosts the internet, and the third-largest digital ad platform in the world.
AMZN.story: Amazon is three businesses bundled into one: a global e-commerce marketplace and logistics network that delivers nearly anything in two days; Amazon Web Services, the largest cloud computing provider in the world and the profit engine of the company; and a fast-growing digital advertising business that has become the third-largest in the world behind only Google and Meta. The AI buildout has supercharged AWS and Amazon's first-party ad business simultaneously.
AMZN.sound_bite: The everything store. Plus the cloud. Plus the ads. Plus the logistics. Each piece would be a top-50 company on its own.

COST.name: Costco Wholesale
COST.big_picture: The bulk-buying retailer with a cult following, built on treating members and employees better than convention allows.
COST.story: Costco operates a membership warehouse club model where customers pay an annual fee for access to bulk products at razor-thin retail margins. The business runs on extraordinary unit economics: members renew at over 90%, the average store generates the highest sales per square foot in retail, and Costco famously pays its employees and treats its customers in ways that build durable trust. The membership fee structure means almost all profit comes from recurring fees, not from individual purchases.
COST.sound_bite: The most loved retailer in America. Membership fees are the business; everything else is the loyalty engine.

TSLA.name: Tesla
TSLA.big_picture: The EV company that became an energy company that became an AI company.
TSLA.story: Tesla is the most valuable automaker in the world by market cap, but the bull case has long since stopped being just about cars. The thesis spans electric vehicles, battery storage and solar (Tesla Energy), Full Self-Driving software, the Optimus humanoid robot, the Dojo training supercomputer, and the eventual Robotaxi network. Whether all of those bets pay off is the central debate of the stock — but the breadth of the optionality is unique.
TSLA.sound_bite: Cars. Energy. Robots. Self-driving. Pick your bet.

SHOP.name: Shopify
SHOP.big_picture: The operating system for online merchants — three million stores worldwide run on it.
SHOP.story: Shopify provides the cloud-based platform that lets entrepreneurs and businesses build, run, and grow online stores. Over three million merchants worldwide use Shopify for their e-commerce — from one-person side hustles to large enterprise brands. The business monetizes through subscription fees and a growing suite of services (payments via Shop Pay, capital, fulfillment, marketing tools).
SHOP.sound_bite: The picks and shovels for everyone selling things online — except Amazon.

DECK.name: Deckers Outdoor
DECK.big_picture: The parent of Hoka and Ugg — two cult footwear brands at very different ends of the market.
DECK.story: Deckers Outdoor owns Hoka, the running shoe brand that went from niche performance footwear to one of the fastest-growing sneaker brands in the world, and Ugg, the cold-weather boot icon that has become a year-round lifestyle brand with a loyal following. The two brands together have made Deckers one of the best-performing consumer names of the last decade.
DECK.sound_bite: Two cult footwear brands, both growing faster than they have any right to.

ROST.name: Ross Stores
ROST.big_picture: Off-price retail — the treasure-hunt model that thrives in good times and great.
ROST.story: Ross Stores runs the off-price treasure-hunt retail model: buy excess inventory from brands and other retailers at a discount, sell it at well below department store prices, and let customers find the deal. The model is recession-resilient and online-resilient (the in-store discovery experience does not translate well to a website).
ROST.sound_bite: When budgets get tight, off-price retail wins. When budgets get loose, off-price retail also wins.

WRBY.name: Warby Parker
WRBY.big_picture: Brought eyewear online with $95 prescription glasses and a vertically integrated brand.
WRBY.story: Warby Parker built the direct-to-consumer eyewear category by selling stylish prescription glasses for $95 — a fraction of what Luxottica's brands charge — and bypassing the traditional optical retail markup. The company started online, then expanded into physical retail (now hundreds of stores), prescriptions, contact lenses, and eye exams.
WRBY.sound_bite: Glasses for $95 instead of $400. Then they opened actual stores.

SN.name: SharkNinja
SN.big_picture: Builds household appliances that punch way above their price point — and a fast-growing global brand.
SN.story: SharkNinja designs and sells household appliances under the Shark and Ninja brands — vacuums, blenders, air fryers, hair tools, cleaning gadgets. The company's playbook is to deliver premium features at mass-market prices with marketing that aggressively positions products against more expensive competitors. International expansion has been a major growth driver.
SN.sound_bite: The vacuum that beats the Dyson at half the price. And the air fryer everyone bought.

CHEF.name: The Chefs' Warehouse
CHEF.big_picture: Specialty food distribution to the country's best independent restaurants.
CHEF.story: The Chefs' Warehouse distributes specialty ingredients — artisanal cheeses, charcuterie, oils, pastries, premium proteins — to the country's best independent restaurants and high-end hotels. The business has grown through acquisition and organic share gains as consolidation reshapes a fragmented foodservice industry.
CHEF.sound_bite: If you ate at a great restaurant in America last week, the cheese probably came from Chefs'.

MAMA.name: Mama's Creations
MAMA.big_picture: Fresh deli-prepared foods sold next to the rotisserie chicken — targeting $1B in revenue by 2030.
MAMA.story: Mama's Creations (formerly MamaMancini's) makes fresh deli-prepared foods — meatballs, meat loaf, chicken, sausage products, pasta and rice entrées — that show up in the prepared-foods section of grocery stores next to the rotisserie chicken. The brand has scaled by adding distribution and acquiring complementary businesses, with a stated goal of $1 billion in annual revenue by 2030.
MAMA.sound_bite: The Italian comfort food brand sitting in the deli case at your grocery store.

NEOG.name: Neogen Corporation
NEOG.big_picture: Food safety, animal health, and biosecurity products for the global food supply chain.
NEOG.story: Neogen makes diagnostic test kits and consumables that food producers, animal health customers, and biosecurity programs use to keep the food supply safe. Tests for pathogens (salmonella, listeria), allergens, mycotoxins, and animal disease screening — the unglamorous infrastructure that keeps the world's food supply trustworthy.
NEOG.sound_bite: Food safety, behind the scenes. The world relies on it without ever noticing.

HLT.name: Hilton Worldwide
HLT.big_picture: One of the world's largest hotel networks, run on a capital-light franchise and management model.
HLT.story: Hilton operates over 8,000 hotels worldwide under brands like Hilton, Waldorf Astoria, Hampton, Embassy Suites, and DoubleTree. Crucially, Hilton owns very few of the buildings — most properties are franchised or managed under contract — which makes the business capital-light, high-margin, and highly cash generative. The Hilton Honors loyalty program has become one of the strongest moats in hospitality.
HLT.sound_bite: Doesn't own the hotels. Owns the brand, the system, and the loyalty program.

RCL.name: Royal Caribbean Group
RCL.big_picture: The cruise giant whose ships are entire destinations — and whose customers keep coming back.
RCL.story: Royal Caribbean operates the largest and newest fleet of cruise ships in the world, including the Icon class — the biggest cruise ships ever built, which function as floating destinations with water parks, restaurants, theaters, and entire neighborhoods. The cruise category has rebounded dramatically post-pandemic, with bookings, pricing, and onboard spending all hitting records.
RCL.sound_bite: When the cruise ship is bigger and more impressive than the destination, you've got Royal Caribbean.

VIK.name: Viking Holdings
VIK.big_picture: Premium river and ocean cruises for adults — no kids, no casinos, all destination focus.
VIK.story: Viking pioneered the modern river cruise category and has expanded into ocean and expedition cruising. The brand is targeted at affluent adults and famously has no children, no casinos, and a strong focus on cultural enrichment at the destinations. Customer loyalty is extraordinary — Viking has waitlists for cabins booked years in advance.
VIK.sound_bite: Cruises for grown-ups who want the Danube, not Daiquiri Night.

LIND.name: Lindblad Expeditions
LIND.big_picture: Polar and adventure expedition travel in partnership with National Geographic.
LIND.story: Lindblad Expeditions runs small-ship expedition cruises to remote destinations — Antarctica, the Arctic, the Galápagos, Alaska — in long-running partnership with National Geographic. The customer base is older, affluent, and willing to pay premium prices for once-in-a-lifetime experiences with naturalists and photographers on board.
LIND.sound_bite: The cruise where the destination is Antarctica and the lecturer is a Nat Geo photographer.

DASH.name: DoorDash
DASH.big_picture: The largest U.S. food delivery platform, expanding into grocery, retail, and international.
DASH.story: DoorDash is the largest food delivery platform in the United States, with significant international expansion through Wolt and other acquisitions. The company has been steadily expanding beyond restaurants into convenience, grocery, alcohol, and general retail delivery. Profitability has improved meaningfully as the business has scaled.
DASH.sound_bite: When nobody wants to leave the house, DoorDash brings dinner.

MELI.name: MercadoLibre
MELI.big_picture: The Amazon of Latin America — plus a fintech business as big as the marketplace.
MELI.story: MercadoLibre is the dominant e-commerce platform across Latin America (Brazil, Argentina, Mexico, and beyond). The company also runs Mercado Pago, a payments and fintech platform that has become a major bank-like business across the region — credit, savings, insurance, and merchant payments. The combination of e-commerce and fintech in markets where both are still under-penetrated is one of the most valuable franchises in emerging markets.
MELI.sound_bite: Two great businesses in one. And in markets where digital commerce is still early.

AS.name: Amer Sports
AS.big_picture: Owns Wilson, Salomon, Arc'teryx, Atomic, and a portfolio of premium sports brands.
AS.story: Amer Sports owns a portfolio of premium technical and outdoor sports brands: Wilson (tennis, basketball, baseball gear), Salomon (running and outdoor), Arc'teryx (luxury technical outerwear), Atomic (skis), and others. Arc'teryx in particular has become a status brand among affluent consumers, with significant pricing power and global expansion runway.
AS.sound_bite: The premium sports brand portfolio that nobody had heard of before Arc'teryx became cool.

CVNA.name: Carvana
CVNA.big_picture: Online used car retailer with the iconic vending machines — turned around dramatically after a near-death experience.
CVNA.story: Carvana sells used cars entirely online, with delivery to the customer's home and the famous glass-tower 'vending machines' as a marketing centerpiece. After nearly going bankrupt in 2022-2023 from over-expansion, the company restructured, cut costs aggressively, and returned to profitability in 2024. The stock has been one of the most extraordinary turnaround stories of the cycle.
CVNA.sound_bite: Came back from the dead. Now selling used cars at scale, profitably.

XPO.name: XPO Inc.
XPO.big_picture: Less-than-truckload (LTL) freight carrier focused on operational excellence and pricing discipline.
XPO.story: XPO is one of the largest less-than-truckload (LTL) freight carriers in North America. After spinning off its other businesses (RXO, GXO), XPO is now a pure-play LTL focused on operational improvement, network density, and pricing discipline. The business is highly cyclical but has structural pricing power as LTL carriers consolidate.
XPO.sound_bite: Pure-play freight at scale. The cycles are real, the pricing power is realer.

AFRM.name: Affirm Holdings
AFRM.big_picture: Buy now, pay later — the alternative to credit cards for major online and in-store purchases.
AFRM.story: Affirm provides point-of-sale installment loans — buy now, pay later — for online and in-store retail purchases. The company underwrites each loan individually and has built deep partnerships with major merchants including Amazon, Walmart, and Apple. Affirm has differentiated itself through transparent pricing (no hidden fees) and disciplined credit underwriting.
AFRM.sound_bite: BNPL with actual underwriting, not just hope.

DAVE.name: Dave Inc.
DAVE.big_picture: Mobile banking app for everyday Americans — no overdraft fees, small-dollar advances, and credit building.
DAVE.story: Dave is a mobile banking app aimed at everyday Americans who pay overdraft fees and live paycheck-to-paycheck. The app offers a checking account with no overdraft fees, small-dollar cash advances against an upcoming paycheck (ExtraCash), and credit-building tools. The business has grown rapidly as consumers seek alternatives to traditional bank fee structures.
DAVE.sound_bite: Banking for the 60% of Americans living paycheck-to-paycheck.

HNGE.name: Hinge Health
HNGE.big_picture: Digital physical therapy for chronic back and joint pain — the largest cause of missed work in America.
HNGE.story: Hinge Health tackles the single largest cause of missed work in America: chronic back and joint pain. Their program works through a smartphone app paired with wearable motion sensors that guide patients through personalized exercises, track form in real time, and connect them with a dedicated coach. The combination of physical therapy, technology, and human support helps people manage pain without surgery or addictive painkillers.
HNGE.sound_bite: Physical therapy through your phone — and it actually works.

APEI.name: American Public Education
APEI.big_picture: Online university serving the U.S. military, federal workforce, and adult learners.
APEI.story: American Public Education runs American Military University and American Public University — the largest online university serving the U.S. military, plus adult learners and the federal workforce. The student base is highly stable (military tuition assistance is a reliable funding source), and the model has scaled to over 100,000 students.
APEI.sound_bite: Higher education for the people who already have full-time jobs — and serve the country.

NFLX.name: Netflix
NFLX.big_picture: Defined and now defends the streaming category — over 300 million subscribers worldwide.
NFLX.story: Netflix is the company that defined streaming entertainment and remains the global leader. With over 300 million subscribers worldwide, the platform generates more revenue and more profit than any other streamer. The recent additions of an ad-supported tier, password-sharing crackdowns, live programming, and gaming have all extended the growth runway. Netflix is profitable in a category where most competitors are still bleeding cash.
NFLX.sound_bite: Won the streaming war. Now monetizing it.

TTWO.name: Take-Two Interactive
TTWO.big_picture: Owns Rockstar Games and 2K Sports — and the entire industry is holding its breath for Grand Theft Auto VI.
TTWO.story: Take-Two Interactive owns Rockstar Games (Grand Theft Auto, Red Dead Redemption) and 2K Sports (NBA 2K, WWE 2K). The entire video game industry is holding its breath for Grand Theft Auto VI, scheduled for 2026, which is widely expected to be the biggest entertainment launch in history. Beyond GTA, Take-Two has a deep portfolio of recurring sports franchises and live-service games.
TTWO.sound_bite: GTA VI in 2026. The most anticipated entertainment launch in history.

LYV.name: Live Nation Entertainment
LYV.big_picture: The global concert business — venues, ticketing through Ticketmaster, festivals, and artist management.
LYV.story: Live Nation Entertainment runs the global concert business through three integrated segments: Concerts (promoting tours and operating venues), Ticketmaster (ticketing technology and distribution), and Sponsorship & Advertising. The company has effectively become the connective tissue of live music, with massive operating leverage on the secular rise in live entertainment spending.
LYV.sound_bite: If you went to a concert in 2025, Live Nation made the money.

IMAX.name: IMAX Corporation
IMAX.big_picture: The premium large-format theater network that movie studios use to make blockbusters feel like events.
IMAX.story: IMAX runs a global network of premium large-format theaters that show the biggest movies on screens designed specifically for that format. The business model is asset-light — IMAX licenses its technology and shares revenue with theater operators rather than owning the theaters. As streaming has eaten most of the movie business, IMAX has become the format where Hollywood concentrates its biggest, most cinematic releases.
IMAX.sound_bite: The screen for the movies that have to be experienced in a theater.

TKO.name: TKO Group Holdings
TKO.big_picture: UFC and WWE under one roof — two of the most valuable combat-sports franchises in the world.
TKO.story: TKO Group was formed in 2023 by combining the UFC (mixed martial arts) and WWE (professional wrestling) under one corporate umbrella. Both franchises have passionate audiences, recurring premium event windows, valuable TV and streaming rights, and pricing power that keeps growing. The combined company has unusual leverage in the broader sports media market.
TKO.sound_bite: UFC and WWE in one company. Two of the most loyal fanbases in entertainment.

MSFT.name: Microsoft
MSFT.big_picture: Software, cloud, and AI giant — Azure, Office 365, and the largest investor in OpenAI.
MSFT.story: Microsoft is one of the most valuable companies in the world, anchored by three franchises: Microsoft 365 (the productivity software almost every business uses), Azure (the second-largest cloud platform, now growing fastest because of AI), and the strategic partnership with OpenAI that put GPT-class models into every Microsoft product. The AI buildout has reaccelerated Azure's growth and turned Microsoft into the most valuable company in tech for periods of 2024 and 2025.
MSFT.sound_bite: Office, Azure, and OpenAI. Three of the biggest franchises in technology, in one company.

GOOGL.name: Alphabet
GOOGL.big_picture: Search, YouTube, Android, Google Cloud, and Waymo — and a Gemini AI program competitive with the best.
GOOGL.story: Alphabet runs Google Search (still the dominant way the world finds information), YouTube (the largest video platform in the world), Android (the most-used mobile operating system), and Google Cloud (the third-largest cloud provider, now growing faster than its bigger rivals). The Gemini family of AI models is competitive with anything from OpenAI or Anthropic. Waymo runs the only true commercial robotaxi service in the United States.
GOOGL.sound_bite: Search, YouTube, Android, Cloud, Gemini, and Waymo. Five franchises any one of which would be a great company.

META.name: Meta Platforms
META.big_picture: Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and the second-largest digital advertising business in the world.
META.story: Meta runs Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger — the largest collection of social platforms in the world, used by billions of people every day. The advertising business is the second-largest in digital ad spend behind only Google. Meta has invested aggressively in AI infrastructure and Reality Labs (AR/VR), funding both from the cash flows of the core ad business.
META.sound_bite: Three billion people use a Meta product daily. Most of them don't realize it.

NET.name: Cloudflare
NET.big_picture: The global network that makes the internet faster and safer — and the platform AI agents are running on.
NET.story: Cloudflare runs one of the world's largest networks, spanning over 330 cities worldwide, with more than 20% of all websites sitting behind it. Think of Cloudflare as a combination security guard and express lane for the internet: it protects websites and apps from cyberattacks while also making them load faster. More recently, Cloudflare Workers has become the go-to infrastructure for AI agents — the automated bots increasingly browsing the web on behalf of people and businesses.
NET.sound_bite: If the internet is the highway and AI agents are the new fleet of trucks, Cloudflare builds the toll roads, guardrails, and service stations they all need.

CRWD.name: CrowdStrike
CRWD.big_picture: Cloud-native cybersecurity built around the Falcon endpoint protection platform.
CRWD.story: CrowdStrike pioneered cloud-native endpoint security with the Falcon platform, which uses lightweight agents on every device combined with a cloud-based AI engine to detect and block threats in real time. The platform has expanded into identity protection, cloud security, and observability — all delivered as modules on the same agent. Despite a major outage in mid-2024, CrowdStrike has maintained customer loyalty and continued growing.
CRWD.sound_bite: The cybersecurity platform the rest of the industry is racing to copy.

DDOG.name: Datadog
DDOG.big_picture: The dominant observability platform — monitoring infrastructure, applications, security, and AI workloads.
DDOG.story: Datadog provides the observability platform that engineers use to monitor their cloud infrastructure, applications, logs, security posture, and increasingly their AI workloads. As cloud usage and complexity grow, observability has become essential — you cannot operate what you cannot see. Datadog has built one of the broadest platforms in the category and lands large enterprise deals through usage-based pricing.
DDOG.sound_bite: When something breaks in the cloud, the first place engineers look is Datadog.

SNOW.name: Snowflake
SNOW.big_picture: The cloud data warehouse that became the foundation for enterprise data and AI.
SNOW.story: Snowflake built the cloud-native data warehouse that separated storage from compute, letting enterprises store unlimited data and pay only for the compute they used. The platform has expanded into data sharing, application development, and AI workloads (Snowflake Cortex). The thesis is that every enterprise needs a single source of truth for its data, and Snowflake wants to be it.
SNOW.sound_bite: The neutral home for enterprise data — and now the foundation for enterprise AI.

PLTR.name: Palantir Technologies
PLTR.big_picture: The data and AI platform for governments and large enterprises — Foundry plus the AIP AI platform.
PLTR.story: Palantir builds Foundry (the data integration and analysis platform used by intelligence agencies, militaries, and large enterprises) and AIP (the AI Platform that lets organizations apply large language models to their internal data).
